$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}=|\bar{a}||\bar{b}| \cos \theta \\ \therefore & \frac{1}{2}=\cos \theta \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \bar{c} \cdot \bar{d}=|\bar{c}||\bar{d}| \cos \phi \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{2}=\cos \phi \Rightarrow \sin \phi=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \left.\left\lvert\, \begin{array}{lll} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{d} \end{array}\right.\right] \left.\bar{c}-\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right] \bar{d} \right\rvert\, \\ & =|((\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \cdot \bar{d}) \bar{c}-((\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \cdot \bar{c}) \bar{d}| \\ & =|(\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \times(\bar{c} \times \bar{d})| \\ & =|\bar{a} \times \bar{b}||\bar{c} \times \bar{d}| \sin \frac{\pi}{6} \\ & =|\bar{a}||\bar{b}| \sin \theta|\bar{c}||\bar{d}| \sin \phi \times \frac{1}{2} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \\ & =\frac{3}{8} \end{array} $$

content: If $\bar{a}=4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \bar{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ then $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})))=$
explanation:
Given:

$$ \vec{a} = 4\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + \hat{k} $$

$$ \vec{b} = \hat{i} - 2\hat{j} + 2\hat{k} $$

We are asked to find:

$$ \vec{a} \times (\vec{a} \times (\vec{a} \times (\vec{a} \times \vec{b}))) $$

Let's proceed step by step.

Step 1: Compute $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$

Use the formula for cross product:

$$ \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 2 \\ \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding determinant:

$ = \hat{i} \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -2 & 2 \\ \end{vmatrix} - \hat{j} \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ \end{vmatrix} + \hat{k} \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 1 & -2 \\ \end{vmatrix} $

$ = \hat{i}[3 \times 2 - 1 \times (-2)] - \hat{j}[4 \times 2 - 1 \times 1] + \hat{k}[4 \times (-2) - 3 \times 1] $

$ = \hat{i}[6 + 2] - \hat{j}[8 - 1] + \hat{k}[-8 - 3] $

$ = 8\hat{i} - 7\hat{j} - 11\hat{k} $

Step 2: Compute $\vec{a} \times (\vec{a} \times \vec{b})$

Let $\vec{c} = \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = 8\hat{i} - 7\hat{j} - 11\hat{k}$

Now,

$ \vec{a} \times \vec{c} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 8 & -7 & -11 \\ \end{vmatrix} $

Expanding:

$ = \hat{i} \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -7 & -11 \\ \end{vmatrix} - \hat{j} \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 8 & -11 \\ \end{vmatrix} + \hat{k} \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 8 & -7 \\ \end{vmatrix} $

$ = \hat{i}[3 \times (-11) - 1 \times (-7)] - \hat{j}[4 \times (-11) - 1 \times 8] + \hat{k}[4 \times (-7) - 3 \times 8] $

$ = \hat{i}[-33 + 7] - \hat{j}[-44 - 8] + \hat{k}[-28 - 24] $

$ = -26\hat{i} + 52\hat{j} - 52\hat{k} $

Step 3: Compute $\vec{a} \times (\vec{a} \times (\vec{a} \times \vec{b}))$

Let $\vec{d} = -26\hat{i} + 52\hat{j} - 52\hat{k}$

$ \vec{a} \times \vec{d} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 3 & 1 \\ -26 & 52 & -52 \\ \end{vmatrix} $

Expanding:

$ = \hat{i} \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 52 & -52 \\ \end{vmatrix} - \hat{j} \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ -26 & -52 \\ \end{vmatrix} + \hat{k} \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ -26 & 52 \\ \end{vmatrix} $

$ = \hat{i}[3 \times (-52) - 1 \times 52] - \hat{j}[4 \times (-52) - 1 \times (-26)] + \hat{k}[4 \times 52 - 3 \times (-26)] $

$ = \hat{i}[-156 - 52] - \hat{j}[-208 + 26] + \hat{k}[208 + 78] $

$ = -208\hat{i} + 182\hat{j} + 286\hat{k} $

Step 4: Compute $\vec{a} \times (\vec{a} \times (\vec{a} \times (\vec{a} \times \vec{b})))$

Let $\vec{e} = -208\hat{i} + 182\hat{j} + 286\hat{k}$

$ \vec{a} \times \vec{e} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 3 & 1 \\ -208 & 182 & 286 \\ \end{vmatrix} $

Expanding:

$ = \hat{i} \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 182 & 286 \\ \end{vmatrix} - \hat{j} \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ -208 & 286 \\ \end{vmatrix} + \hat{k} \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ -208 & 182 \\ \end{vmatrix} $

$ = \hat{i}[3 \times 286 - 1 \times 182] - \hat{j}[4 \times 286 - 1 \times (-208)] + \hat{k}[4 \times 182 - 3 \times (-208)] $

$ = \hat{i}[858 - 182] - \hat{j}[1144 + 208] + \hat{k}[728 + 624] $

$ = \hat{i}[676] - \hat{j}[1352] + \hat{k}[1352] $

$ = 676\hat{i} - 1352\hat{j} + 1352\hat{k} $

Now, observe that

$ \vec{b} = \hat{i} - 2\hat{j} + 2\hat{k} $

$ 676\vec{b} = 676\hat{i} - 1352\hat{j} + 1352\hat{k} $

So, the answer is

$ \boxed{676\;\vec{b}} $

This matches Option B.

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ are unit vectors and $\theta$ is the angle between them, then $\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ is a unit vector when $\theta$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & |\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=1 \\ \therefore & |\vec{a}+\vec{b}|^2=1^2 \\ \therefore & |\vec{a}|^2+2 \vec{a} \cdot \bar{b}+|\vec{b}|^2=1 \\ \therefore & 1+2 \vec{a} \cdot \bar{b}+1=1 \\ \therefore & \bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}=\frac{-1}{2} \\ \therefore & |\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{2} \\ \therefore & \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{2} \\ \therefore & \theta=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \end{array} $$

content: The magnitude of a vector which is orthogonal to the vector $\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and is coplanar with the vectors $\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ and $\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ is
explanation:
Let $\bar{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \hat{b}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ and $\bar{c}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$

By definition, vector orthogonal to $\bar{a}$ and coplanar to $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is given by $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{c}) \bar{b}-(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}) \bar{c} \\ & =4(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})-4(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}) \\ & =4(-\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Magnitude }=\sqrt{4^2\left(1^2+1^2\right)}=4 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: Let $\overline{\mathrm{OA}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{OB}}=\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and if the vector along the angle bisector of $\angle \mathrm{AOB}$ is given by $x \frac{\overline{\mathrm{a}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|}+y \frac{\overline{\mathrm{~b}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|}$ then
explanation:
$\overline{\mathrm{OA}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{OB}}=\overline{\mathrm{b}}$

…[Given]

Direction of the angle bisector of $\angle A O B$ is given by,

$$ \vec{v}=\frac{\bar{a}}{|\bar{a}|}+\frac{\bar{b}}{|\bar{b}|} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

But, angle bisector is given in the form

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=x \cdot \frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}}{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|}+y \cdot \frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}}{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}|} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & x=1 \text { and } y=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x-y=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: In triangle ABC , the point P divides BC internally in the ratio $3: 4$ and Q divides CA internally in the ratio $5: 3$. If AP and BQ intersect in a point $G$, then $G$ divides $A P$ internally in the ratio
explanation:
Let the vertices of triangle ABC be $\mathrm{A}=(0,0), \mathrm{B}=(1,0), \mathrm{C}=(0,1) P$ divides $B C$ in the ratio $3: 4$ and $Q$ divides CA in the ratio $5: 3$.

By using section formula, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & P=\left(\frac{3 \times 0+4 \times 1}{3+4}, \frac{3 \times 1+4 \times 0}{3+4}\right)=\left(\frac{4}{7}, \frac{3}{7}\right) \\ & Q=\left(\frac{5 \times 0+3 \times 0}{5+3}, \frac{5 \times 0+3 \times 1}{5+3}\right)=\left(0, \frac{3}{8}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Point on AP is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & (x, y)=(1-t) \cdot(0,0)+t \cdot\left(\frac{4}{7}, \frac{3}{7}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad(x, y)=\left(\frac{4 t}{7}, \frac{3 t}{7}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Point on BQ is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & (x, y)=(1-\mathrm{s}) \cdot(1,0)+\mathrm{s} \cdot\left(0, \frac{3}{8}\right) \\ &\therefore (x, y)=\left(1-\mathrm{s}, \frac{3 \mathrm{~s}}{8}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \left(\frac{4 \mathrm{t}}{7}, \frac{3 \mathrm{t}}{7}\right)=\left(1-\mathrm{s}, \frac{3 \mathrm{~s}}{8}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{4 \mathrm{t}}{7}=1-\mathrm{s} \ldots \text { (i) and } \frac{3 \mathrm{t}}{7}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~s}}{8}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii) \end{aligned} $$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$ t=\frac{7}{12} $$

Now,

$$ \begin{aligned} A G: G P=t:(1-t) & =\frac{7}{12}:\left(1-\frac{7}{12}\right) \\ & =\frac{7}{12}: \frac{5}{12} \\ & =7: 5 \end{aligned} $$

content: Let $\bar{u}, \bar{v}, \bar{w}$ be the vectors such that $|\overline{\mathrm{u}}|=1,|\overline{\mathrm{v}}|=2,|\overline{\mathrm{w}}|=3$. If the projection $\overline{\mathrm{v}}$ along $\overline{\mathrm{u}}$ is equal to that of $\overline{\mathrm{w}}$ along $\overline{\mathrm{u}}$ and the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{v}}, \overline{\mathrm{w}}$ are perpendicular to each other then $|\overline{\mathrm{u}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}}+\overline{\mathrm{w}}|$ equals
explanation:
According to the given condition, Projection of $\bar{v}$ on $\bar{u}=$ projection of $\bar{w}$ on $\bar{u}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{u}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{u}}|}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{w}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{u}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{u}}|} \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{u}}=\overline{\mathrm{w}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{u}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Vectors $\overline{\mathrm{v}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{w}}$ are perpendicular to each other.

$$ \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{w}}=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Consider, }\\ &\begin{aligned} |\overline{\mathrm{u}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}}+\overline{\mathrm{w}}|^2=|\overline{\mathrm{u}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{v}}|^2 & +|\overline{\mathrm{w}}|^2 -2 \overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{v}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{w}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{w}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{lll} & =(1)^2+(2)^2+(3)^2 \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) and (ii) }] \\ & =1+4+9 \\ \therefore & |\overline{\mathrm{u}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}}+\overline{\mathrm{w}}|^2=14 \\ \therefore & |\overline{\mathrm{u}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}}+\overline{\mathrm{w}}|=\sqrt{14} \end{array} $$

content: The projection of the line segment joining the points $(2,1,-3)$ and $(-1,0,2)$ on the line whose direction ratios are $3,2,6$ is
explanation:
Let $\vec{A}=(2,1,-3)$ and $\vec{B}=(-1,0,2)$

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=(-3,-1,5) $$

Let $\vec{a}=\overrightarrow{A B}$ and $\vec{b}=(3,2,6)$

Projection of $\vec{a}$ on $\vec{b}=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{b}|}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{(-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{3^2+2^2+6^2}} \\ & =\frac{(-3)(3)+(-1)(2)+(5)(6)}{\sqrt{9+4+36}} \\ & =\frac{-9-2+30}{\sqrt{49}} \\ & =\frac{19}{7} \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\bar{a}, \bar{b}, \bar{c}$ are three vectors such that $|\bar{a}|=3$, $|\bar{b}|=5,|\bar{c}|=7$ then $|\bar{a}-\bar{b}|^2+|\bar{b}-\bar{c}|^2+|\bar{c}-\bar{a}|^2$ does not exceed
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Given, }|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=3,|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|=5,|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=7 \\ & |\overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2 \\ & =|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2-2 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2-2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2 -2c\bar\,\cdot \bar{a}\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =2\left(|\bar{a}|^2+|\bar{b}|^2+|\bar{c}|^2\right)-2(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}+\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c}+\bar{c} \cdot \bar{a}) \\ & =2(9+25+49)-2(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}+\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c}+\bar{c} \cdot \bar{a}) \\ & =2(83)-2(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}+\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c}+\bar{c} \cdot \bar{a}) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Now, $|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}|^2 \geq 0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 83+2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}} \geq \frac{-83}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow-2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \leq 83 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$ |\overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2 \leq 166+83 \leq 249 $$

content: If $\left[\begin{array}{lll}2 \bar{p}-3 \bar{r} & \bar{q} & \bar{s}\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{lll}3 \bar{p}+2 \bar{q} & \bar{r} & \bar{s}\end{array}\right]=m\left[\begin{array}{lll}\bar{p} & \bar{r} & \bar{s}\end{array}\right] +n\left[\begin{array}{lll}\bar{q} & \bar{r} & \bar{s}\end{array}\right]+t\left[\begin{array}{lll}\bar{p} & \bar{q} & \bar{s}\end{array}\right]$, then the values of $\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n}, \mathrm{t}$ respectively are ....
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\begin{array}{lll} 2 \bar{p}-3 \bar{r} & \bar{q} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 \bar{p}+2 \bar{q} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]} \\ & =\mathrm{m}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{p} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+\mathrm{n}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{q} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+\mathrm{t}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{p} & \bar{q} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right] \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{lll} 2 \bar{p} & \bar{q} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{3 r} & \bar{q} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 \bar{p} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]} \\ & \quad+\left[\begin{array}{lll} 2 \bar{q} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{r} =\mathrm{m}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{p}} & \overline{\mathrm{r}} & \overline{\mathrm{~s}} \end{array}\right]+\mathrm{n}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{q}} & \overline{\mathrm{r}} & \overline{\mathrm{~s}} \end{array}\right]+\mathrm{t}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{p}} & \overline{\mathrm{q}} & \overline{\mathrm{~s}} \end{array}\right] \\ 3\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{p}} & \overline{\mathrm{r}} & \overline{\mathrm{~s}} \end{array}\right]+3\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{q}} & \overline{\mathrm{r}} & \overline{\mathrm{~s}} \end{array}\right]+2\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{q}} & \overline{\mathrm{r}} & \overline{\mathrm{~s}} \end{array}\right] \\ +\left[\begin{array}{lll} 2 \overline{\mathrm{p}} & \overline{\mathrm{q}} & \overline{\mathrm{~s}} \end{array}\right] \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & =m\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{p} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+n\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{q} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+t\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{p} & \bar{q} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right] \\ & 3\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{p} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+5\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{q} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+2\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{p} & \bar{q} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right] \\ & =m\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{p} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+n\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{q} & \bar{r} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right]+t\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{p} & \bar{q} & \bar{s} \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Comparing above equations, we get }\\ &\mathrm{m}=3, \mathrm{n}=5, \mathrm{t}=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The volume of the tetrahedron whose co-terminus edges are $\bar{a}, \bar{b}, \bar{c}$ is 12 cubic units. If the scalar projection of $\bar{a}$ on $\bar{b} \times \bar{c}$ is 4 , then $|\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}|=$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Volume of tetrahedron }=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right]=72 \\ & \Rightarrow \bar{a} \cdot(\bar{b} \times \bar{c})=72 \quad\text{..... (i)}\\ & \text { Scalar projection of } \bar{a} \text { on } \bar{b} \times \bar{c}=\frac{\bar{a} \cdot(b \times c)}{|\bar{b} \times \bar{c}|} \\ & 4=\frac{\bar{a} \cdot(\bar{b} \times \bar{c})}{|\bar{b} \times \bar{c}|} \\ & \Rightarrow 4=\frac{72}{|\bar{b} \times \bar{c}|} \quad . ..[\text { From (i) }]\\ & \Rightarrow|\bar{b} \times \bar{c}|=\frac{72}{4}=18 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ are two vectors, then the angle between the vectors $3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+5 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}$ and $5 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} 3 \bar{a}+5 \bar{b} & =3(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+5(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \\ & =13 \hat{i}+9 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k} \\ 5 \bar{a}+3 \bar{b} & =5(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+3(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \\ & =11 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+12 \hat{k} \\ \therefore \quad \cos \theta & =\frac{(13 \hat{i}+9 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \cdot(11 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+12 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{169+81+16} \sqrt{121+1+144}} \\ & =\frac{13(11)+9(-1)+1(12)}{\sqrt{266} \sqrt{266}} \\ & =\frac{143-9+48}{266} \\ & =\frac{182}{266} \\ & =\frac{13}{19}\\ \therefore\quad& \theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{13}{19}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ is perpendicular to $\bar{b}$ and $\bar{c},|\bar{a}|=2$, $|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=3,|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=4$ and the angle between $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is $\frac{\pi}{3}$, then $\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{b}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}}\end{array}\right]=$
explanation:
Let $\hat{\mathrm{n}}$ be the unit vector perpendicular to $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\bar{c}$.

$$\begin{aligned} {\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{~b}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \end{array}\right] } & =\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \\ & =\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}| \sin \theta \hat{\mathrm{n}}) \\ & =\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot\left(3 \times 4 \sin \frac{\pi}{3} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}\right) \\ & =\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot\left(12 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{n}}\right) \\ & =6 \sqrt{3}|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\hat{\mathrm{n}}| \cos 0 \\ & =6 \sqrt{3} \times 2 \times 1 \\ & =12 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \bar{b}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$ and $\bar{c}=4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$, then the unit vector in the direction of $3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & 3 \bar{a}+\bar{b}-2 \bar{c} \\ & =3(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})+(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k})-2(4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}) \\ & =-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { The unit vector in the direction of } 3 \bar{a}+\bar{b}-2 \bar{c} \text { is }\\ &\frac{-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}}{|-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}|} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{(-1)^2+2^2+(-1)^2}} \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \quad \bar{b}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\bar{c}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}$ are the vectors such that $\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}}$ is perpendicular to $\bar{c}$, then value of $\lambda$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\lambda(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & \quad=(2-\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(2+2 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(3+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & (\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(2-\lambda)(3)+(2+2 \lambda)(1)+(3+\lambda)(0)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 8-\lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=8 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$ are three vectors such that $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \neq \overline{0}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=2 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}},|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=1,|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|=4$ and $|\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}|=\sqrt{15}$. If $\overline{\mathrm{b}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\lambda \overline{\mathrm{a}}$, then $\lambda$ is
explanation:
If angle between $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is $\alpha$ and

$$\begin{aligned} & |\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=\sqrt{15} \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \sin \alpha=\sqrt{15} \\ & \Rightarrow(4)(1) \sin \alpha=\sqrt{15} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \alpha=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \alpha=\frac{1}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $\overline{\mathrm{b}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\lambda \overline{\mathrm{a}}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{b}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=\lambda^2|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2 \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|^2+4|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2-4 \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\lambda^2|\vec{a}|^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 16+4-4(|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}||\bar{c}| \cos \alpha)=\lambda^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 20-4\left(4 \times 1 \times \frac{1}{4}\right)=\lambda^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 16=\lambda^2\\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\pm4 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ are unit coplanar vectors, then the scalar triple product $\left[\begin{array}{lll}2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}} & 2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}} & 2 \overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}\end{array}\right]$ has the value
explanation:
$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$ are coplanar vectors.

$$\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{~b}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \end{array}\right]=0$$

Let $\bar{\alpha}=2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}, \bar{\beta}=2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ and $\gamma=2 \overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}$. Then,

$$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{\alpha} & \bar{\beta} & \bar{\gamma} \end{array}\right]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & 2 \end{array}\right| \cdot\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right]} \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{\alpha} & \bar{\beta} & \bar{\gamma} \end{array}\right]=7\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{~b}} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right]=7(0)=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$ be such that $|\bar{a}|=2,|\bar{b}|=4$ and $|\bar{c}|=4$. If the projection of $\bar{b}$ on $\bar{a}$ is equal to the projection of $\bar{c}$ on $\bar{a}$ and $\bar{b}$ is perpendicular to $\bar{c}$, then the value of $|\vec{a}+\bar{b}-\bar{c}|$ is
explanation:
$$|\vec{a}|=2,|\vec{b}|=4,|\bar{c}|=4$$

According to the given condition,

Projection of $\bar{b}$ on $\bar{a}=$ projection of $\bar{c}$ on $\bar{a}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}}{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|} \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}} \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{~b}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, }|\vec{a}+\bar{b}-\bar{c}|=\sqrt{|\bar{a}+\bar{b}-\bar{c}|^2} \\ & =\sqrt{|\bar{a}|^2+|\vec{b}-\bar{c}|^2+2 \bar{a} \cdot(\bar{b}-\bar{c})} \\ & =\sqrt{(2)^2+|\bar{b}-\bar{c}|^2+2(0)} \quad\text{.... [From (i)]}\\ & =\sqrt{4+|\bar{b}-\bar{c}|^2} \\ & =\sqrt{4+|\bar{b}|^2+|\bar{c}|^2-2(\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c})} \\ & =\sqrt{4+4^2+4^2-2(0)}\quad \ldots[\overline{\mathrm{b}} \text { is perpendicular to } \overline{\mathrm{c}}]\\ & =\sqrt{36}=6 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ be three non-zero vectors such that no two of them are collinear and $(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\mathrm{c}| \overline{\mathrm{a}}$. If $\theta$ is the angle between vectors $\bar{b}$ and $\bar{c}$, then the value of $\sin \theta$ is
explanation:
Given, $(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \overline{\mathrm{a}}$

We know that,

$$(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}$$

On comparing, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=-\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=-|\overline{\mathrm{b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{1}{9} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \sin ^2 \theta & =1-\cos ^2 \theta \\ & =1-\frac{1}{9} \\ \therefore \quad \sin ^2 \theta & =\frac{8}{9} \\ \therefore \quad \sin \theta & =\sqrt{\frac{8}{9}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}} & \overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}\end{array}\right]=\lambda\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{b}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}}\end{array}\right]^2$, then $\lambda$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & {[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}] } \\ & =(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot\{(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \times(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})\} \\ & =(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot\{((\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}}-((\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}\} \\ & =(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot\{((\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}}\} \ldots[\because(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=0] \\ & =\{(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}\}((\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \\ & =[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}][\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}] \ldots \ldots[\because[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=[\overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{a}}]] \\ & =[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]^2 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore\quad \lambda =1$

content: If the vectors $\overline{A B}=3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{k}$ and $\overline{A C}=5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$ are the sides of the triangle $A B C$, then the length of the median, through $A$, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let } \mathrm{AD} \text { be the median of } \triangle \mathrm{ABC} \text {. }\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{AD}} & =\frac{\overline{\mathrm{AB}}+\overline{\mathrm{AC}}}{2} \\ & =\frac{3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{2} \\ & =\frac{8 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+8 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{2} \\ & =4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore|\overline{\mathrm{AD}}|=\sqrt{4^2+(-1)^2+4^2}=\sqrt{33}$ units

content: If $\bar{a}$ and $\bar{b}$ are two unit vectors such that $\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}$ and $5 \overline{\mathrm{a}}-4 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}$ are perpendicular to each other, then the angle between $\bar{a}$ and $\bar{b}$ is
explanation:
Let $\theta$ be the angle between $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$.

Since $\bar{c}=\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}$ and $\bar{d}=5 \bar{a}-4 \bar{b}$ are perpendicular to each other.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~d}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}) \cdot(5 \overline{\mathrm{a}}-4 \overline{\mathrm{~b}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}})+6(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}})-8(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+6|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta-8|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5+6 \cos \theta-8=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $P, Q, R$ and $S$ be the points on the plane with position vectors $-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}, 4 \hat{i}, 3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$ and $-3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}$ respectively. Then the quadrilateral PQRS must be a

options.content: parallelogram, which is neither a rhombus nor a rectangle.

explanation:
$$\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{PQ}}=\frac{1}{6}, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{SR}}=\frac{1}{6}, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{RQ}}=-3, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{SP}}=-3$$

$\square \mathrm{PQRS}$ is a parallelogram.

But neither $\mathrm{PR}=\mathrm{SQ}$ nor $\mathrm{PR} \perp \mathrm{SQ}$.

$\therefore \quad$ Parallelogram, which is neither a rhombus nor a rectangle.

content: If the points $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}$ and R are with the position vectors $\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k},-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ and $-8 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}$ respectively, then these points are
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \overline{\mathrm{PQ}}= & -2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}-(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \\ & =-3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-\hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \overline{Q R} & =-8 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}-(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \\ & =-6 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} \\ & =2(-3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-\hat{k})=2 \overline{P Q} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{QR}}$ is a scalar multiple of $\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}$.

$\therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{QR}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}$ are parallel to each other with point Q in common.

$\therefore \quad$ Points $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}$ and R are collinear and Q lies between P and R .

content: One side and one diagonal of a parallelogram are represented by $3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$ respectively, then the area of parallelogram in square units is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}} \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k} \\ & \text { In } \triangle \mathrm{ABC} \text {, } \\ & \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}=\overline{\mathrm{c}} \\ & \text {...[Using triangle law of addition] } \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}} \\ & =2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}-(3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})=-\hat{i}-\hat{k} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 3 & 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & -1 \end{array}\right|=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Area of parallelogram }=|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}| \\ & =\sqrt{1+16+1} \\ & =\sqrt{18}=3 \sqrt{2} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: If the vector $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ lies in the plane of $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$, where $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}=x \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(2-x) \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$, then the value of $x$ is
explanation:
According to the given condition, vectors $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$ are coplanar.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=0 \\ & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ x & x-2 & -1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 1(-1-x+2)+1(-1-x)+2(x-2-x)=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 1-x-1-x-4=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 2 x=-4 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow x=-2 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ be three vectors. A vector $\bar{v}$ in the plane of $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$, whose projection on $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, is given by
explanation:
$$\bar{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \bar{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k} \text { and } \bar{c}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}$$

$\overline{\mathrm{v}}$ is in the plane of $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{m} \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\mathrm{n} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} . \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{v}}=(m+n) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(m-n) \hat{j}+(m+n) \hat{\mathrm{k}}\quad\text{... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Projection of $\bar{v}$ on $\bar{c}=\frac{\bar{v} \cdot \bar{c}}{|\bar{c}|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{(m+n)(1)+(m-n)(-1)+(m+n)(-1)}{\sqrt{1+1+1}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \Rightarrow-m+n=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=1+\mathrm{m} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{v}}=(2 \mathrm{~m}+1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2 \mathrm{~m}+1) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) }] \end{aligned}$$

When $\mathrm{m}=1$ then $\overline{\mathrm{v}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ are two unit vectors such that $5 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+4 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{a}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}$ are perpendicular to each other, then the angle between $\bar{a}$ and $\bar{b}$ is
explanation:
Let $\theta$ be the angle between $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$.

Since $5 \bar{a}+4 \bar{b}$ and $\bar{a}-2 \bar{b}$ are perpendicular to each other.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & (5 \bar{a}+4 \bar{b}) \cdot(\bar{a}-2 \bar{b})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{a})-6(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b})-8(\bar{b} \cdot \bar{b})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2-6|\bar{a}||\bar{b}| \cos \theta-8|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5-6 \cos \theta-8=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$ and $\bar{b}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}$. If $\bar{c}$ is a vector such that $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|,|\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=2 \sqrt{2}$ and the angle between $(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is $30^{\circ}$, then $|(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}|$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}} =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right| \\ & =2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad & |\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|=\sqrt{4+4+1}=3 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & |\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=2 \sqrt{2} \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}})^2=8 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2-2 \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=8 \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+9-2|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=8 \quad \ldots[\because \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|]\\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2-2|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|-\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|] \\ & \Rightarrow \mid \overline{\mathrm{c}}=1\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, }|(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}| \\ & =|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot|\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \sin 30^{\circ} \\ & =(3)(1)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \quad\text{.... [From (i) and (ii)]}\\ & =\frac{3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{pi}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{q} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ are mutually orthogonal, then $(p, q)$ is equal to
explanation:
Since the given vectors are mutually orthogonal.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \bar{a} \cdot \bar{b} & =2-4+2 \\ \bar{a} \cdot \bar{c} & =p-1+2 q=0 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ \bar{b} \cdot \bar{c} & =2 p+4+q=0\quad\text{..... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$(p, q)=(-3,2)$$

content: If $\bar{u}, \bar{v}$ and $\bar{w}$ are three non-coplanar vectors, then $(\bar{u}+\bar{v}-\bar{w}) \cdot[(\bar{u}-\bar{v}) \times(\bar{v}-\bar{w})]$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{(\bar{u}+\bar{v}-\bar{w}) \cdot[(\bar{u}-\bar{v}) \times(\bar{v}-\bar{w})]} \\ & =\overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{u}} \times \overline{\mathrm{v}})-\overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{u}} \times \overline{\mathrm{w}})+\overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{v}} \times \overline{\mathrm{w}})+\overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{u}} \times \overline{\mathrm{v}}) \\ & -\overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{u}} \times \overline{\mathrm{w}})+\overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{v}} \times \overline{\mathrm{w}})-\overline{\mathrm{w}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{u}} \times \overline{\mathrm{v}}) \\ & \mathrm{+\bar{w} \cdot(\bar{u} \times \bar{w})-\bar{w} \cdot(\bar{v} \times \bar{w})} \\ & \mathrm{=[\bar{u} \bar{v} \bar{w}]+[\bar{v} \bar{u} \bar{w}]-[\bar{w} \bar{u} \bar{v}]} \\ & \mathrm{=[\bar{u} \bar{v} \bar{w}]+[\bar{u} \bar{v} \bar{w}]-[\bar{u} \bar{v} \bar{w}]} \\ & \mathrm{=\overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{v}} \times \overline{\mathrm{w}})} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\overline{\mathrm{u}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}, \overline{\mathrm{v}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{w}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If $\hat{\mathrm{n}}$ is a unit vector such that $\overline{\mathbf{u}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}=0$ and $\overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}=0$, then $|\overline{\mathrm{w}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}|$ is equal to
explanation:
We have, $\overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}=0$ and $\overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}=0$

$\therefore \quad \hat{\mathrm{n}} \perp \overline{\mathrm{u}}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{n}} \perp \overline{\mathrm{v}}$

$$\Rightarrow \hat{\mathbf{n}}= \pm \frac{\overline{\mathrm{u}} \times \overline{\mathrm{v}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{u}} \times \overline{\mathrm{v}}|}$$

Now, $\overline{\mathrm{u}} \times \overline{\mathrm{v}}=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}) \times(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}})=-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$

$\therefore \quad \hat{\mathbf{n}}= \pm \hat{\mathrm{k}}$

Hence, $|\bar{w} \cdot \hat{n}|=|(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot( \pm \hat{k})|=3$

content: Let $\bar{a}, \bar{b}$ and $\bar{c}$ be three unit vectors such that $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(\overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})$. If $\bar{b}$ is not parallel to $\bar{c}$, then the angle between $\bar{a}$ and $\bar{b}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(\overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \overline{\mathrm{c}} \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}=\cos \frac{5 \pi}{6} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{5 \pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\hat{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}(3 \hat{i}+\hat{k})$ and $\hat{b}=\frac{1}{7}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k})$, then the value of $(2 \hat{a}-\hat{b}) \cdot[(\hat{a} \times \hat{b}) \times(\hat{a}+2 \hat{b})]$ is
explanation:
Here, $\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=0$

$\therefore \quad \hat{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{b}}$ are perpendicular unit vectors.

Now, $(2 \hat{a}-\hat{b}) \cdot\{(\hat{a} \times \hat{b}) \times(\hat{a}+2 \hat{b})\}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 \hat{a}-\hat{b} \quad \hat{a} \times \hat{b} \quad \hat{a}+2 \hat{b} \end{array}\right] \\ & =-\left[\begin{array}{ll} \hat{a} \times \hat{b} & 2 \hat{a}-\hat{b} \quad \hat{a}+2 \hat{b}] \end{array}\right. \\ & =-(\hat{a} \times \hat{b}) \cdot\{(2 \hat{a}-\hat{b}) \times(\hat{a}+2 \hat{b})\} \\ & =-(\hat{a} \times \hat{b}) \cdot 5(\hat{a} \times \hat{b}) \\ & =-5|\hat{a} \times \hat{b}|=-5|\hat{a}|^2|\hat{b}|^2 \quad \ldots[\because \hat{a} \perp \hat{b}]\\ & =-5\quad \cdots[\because|\hat{a}|=|\hat{b}|=1] \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}$, and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ be three non-zero vectors such that no two of these are collinear. If the vector $\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}$ is collinear with $\bar{c}$ and $\bar{b}+3 \bar{c}$ is collinear with $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$, then $\overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+6 \overline{\mathrm{c}}$ equals
explanation:
$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}$ is collinear with $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$

$\therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}=\mathrm{nc}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

Similarly $\overline{\mathrm{b}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{ma}\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

m and n are non-zero scalars.

$\therefore \quad$ (i) $\Rightarrow \bar{a}+2 \bar{b}+6 \bar{c}=(n+6) \bar{c}$

(ii) $\Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+6 \overline{\mathrm{c}}=(2 \mathrm{~m}+1) \overline{\mathrm{a}}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}+6=0$ and $2 \mathrm{~m}+1=0$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=-6$ and $\mathrm{m}=\frac{-1}{2}$

$\therefore \quad$ (i) $\Rightarrow \bar{a}+2 \bar{b}+6 \bar{c}=0$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$ are non-coplanar unit vectors such that $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=\frac{(\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})}{\sqrt{2}}$ then the angle between $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\bar{b}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-\left(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \overline{\mathrm{c}}=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Since } \overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{~b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}} \text { are non-coplanar unit vectors, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{3 \pi}{4} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of unit vectors perpendicular to $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=(1,1,0)$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}=(0,1,1)$ is
explanation:
The vector perpendicular to $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ is

$$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

Since the length of this vector is $\sqrt{3}$, the unit vector perpendicular to $\bar{a}$ and $\bar{b}$ is $\pm \frac{\bar{a} \times \bar{b}}{|\bar{a} \times \bar{b}|}= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})$

Hence, there are two such vectors.

content: If the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\lambda \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mu \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ are mutually orthogonal, then $(\lambda, \mu) \equiv$
explanation:
As vectors $\bar{a}, \bar{b}, \bar{c}$ are mutually orthogonal, we get

$$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=0$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \lambda-1+2 \mu=0 \\ \therefore & \lambda+2 \mu=1\quad\text{... (i)} \end{array}$$

and $\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c}=0$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 \lambda+4+\mu=0 \\ \therefore & 2 \lambda+\mu=-4\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{array}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$\lambda=-3, \mu=2$$

content: Let $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ be three non-zero vectors such that no two of them are collinear and $(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \overline{\mathrm{a}}$. If ' $\theta$ ' is the angle between the vectors $\bar{b}$ and $\bar{c}$, then value of $\sin \theta$ is
explanation:
Given: $(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \overline{\mathrm{a}}$

We know that,

$$(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}$$

On comparing, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=-\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=-|\overline{\mathrm{b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{1}{9} \\ & \sin ^2 \theta=1-\cos ^2 \theta \\ & \quad=1-\frac{1}{9} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore\quad& \sin ^2 \theta=\frac{8}{9} \\ \therefore\quad& \sin \theta=\sqrt{\frac{8}{9}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\bar{x}=\frac{\bar{b} \times \bar{c}}{[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]}, \bar{y}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}}{[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{z}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}}{[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]}$ where $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$ are non-coplanar vectors, then value of $\bar{x} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}})+\bar{y} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})+\overline{\mathrm{z}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}})$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \bar{x} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}})+\bar{y} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})+\overline{\mathrm{z}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \\ & =(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot \bar{x}+(\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot \bar{y}+(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{z}} \\ & =\frac{[\mathrm{a} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]+0+[\overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{a}}]+0+[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{a} \bar{b}]+0}{[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]} \\ & =3 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\bar{a}$ and $\bar{b}$ are two unit vectors such that $5 \bar{a}+4 \bar{b}$ and $\bar{a}-2 \bar{b}$ are perpendicular to each other, then the between $\bar{a}$ and $\bar{b}$ is
explanation:
Let $\theta$ be the angle between $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$.

Since $\bar{c}=\bar{a}-2 \bar{b}$ and $\bar{d}=5 \bar{a}+4 \bar{b} \quad$ are perpendicular to each other.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~d}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}) \cdot(5 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+4 \overline{\mathrm{~b}})=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 5(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}})-6(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}})-8(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2-6|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta-8|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 5-6 \cos \theta-8=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \cos \theta=-\frac{1}{2} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let two non-collinear unit vectors $\hat{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{b}}$ form an acute angle. A point P moves, so that at any time $t$ the position vector $\overline{O P}$, where $O$ is the origin, is given by $\hat{a} \cos t+\hat{b} \sin t$. When $P$ is farthest from origin O , let M be the length of $\overline{\mathrm{OP}}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{u}}$ be the unit vector along $\overline{\mathrm{OP}}$, then
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} M & =|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}| \\ M & =\hat{\mathrm{a}} \cos t+\hat{b} \sin t \\ & =\sqrt{(\hat{a} \cos t)^2+(\hat{b} \sin t)^2+2(\hat{a} \cos t) \cdot(\hat{b} \cdot \sin t)} \\ & =\sqrt{\sin ^2 t+\cos ^2 t+\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}(2 \sin t \cos t)} \\ & =\sqrt{1+\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}(\sin 2 t)} \end{aligned}$$

Maximum value of $\sin 2 t=1$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 \mathrm{t}=\sin ^{-1}(1) \\ \therefore & \mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ \therefore & M=\sqrt{1+\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}(1)}=(1+\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b})^{\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}$$

Now, $\hat{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{OP}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{OP}}|}$

$$\begin{aligned} &=\frac{\hat{a} \cos t+\hat{b} \sin t}{|\hat{a} \sin t+\hat{b} \sin t|}=\frac{\hat{a}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\hat{b}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}{\left|\hat{a}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\hat{b}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right|}\\ &\text { Unit vector of OP is }\\ &\hat{u}=\frac{\hat{a}+\hat{b}}{|(\hat{a}+\hat{b})|} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$ are mutually perpendicular vectors having magnitudes $1,2,3$ respectively, then the value of $\left[\begin{array}{lll}\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c} & \bar{b}-\bar{a} & \bar{c}\end{array}\right]$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & |\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=1,|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|=2,|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=3 \\ & {[\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}} \quad \overline{\mathrm{c}}]} \\ & =(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{b}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}] \\ & =(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \\ & =[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]-[\overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}] \\ & =2[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}] \\ & =2 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \\ & =2|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}| \cos 0^{\circ} \quad \cdots\left[\begin{array}{l} \because \overline{\mathrm{a}} \perp \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{c}} \\ \therefore \overline{\mathrm{a}} \| \overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}} \end{array}\right]\\ & =2|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}| \\ & =2|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \sin 90^{\circ} \quad \cdots[\because \overline{\mathrm{b}} \perp \overline{\mathrm{c}}]\\ & =2 \times 1 \times 2 \times 3 \\ & =12 \end{aligned}$$

content: The vector of magnitude 6 units and perpendicular to vectors $2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ is
explanation:
Let the required vector be $\bar{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}$.

Then, $|\hat{H}|=6$

$$\Rightarrow x^2+y^2+z^2=36\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Now, $\bar{r}$ is perpendicular to vectors $\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$ and $\bar{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$.

$$\therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=-5(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})$$

$$\therefore \quad x=y=z$$

Let $x=y=z=\lambda$

Let $x=y=z=\lambda$

From (i), we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & 3 \lambda^2=36 \\ \therefore \quad & \lambda=2 \sqrt{3} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Required vector is } 2 \sqrt{3}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) . \end{array}$$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$, then the vector $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ satisfying $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\overline{0}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}=3$ is
explanation:
Let's determine the vector $$\mathbf{b}=(b_1, b_2, b_3)$$ satisfying the two equations:

$$\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0},$$

$$\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 3.$$

We are given:

$$\mathbf{a} = \hat{j} - \hat{k} = (0,1,-1),$$

$$\mathbf{c} = \hat{i} - \hat{j} - \hat{k} = (1,-1,-1).$$

Step 1. Expressing the cross product:

The cross product $$\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}$$ for $$\mathbf{a}=(0,1,-1)$$ and $$\mathbf{b}=(b_1, b_2, b_3)$$ is computed by:

$$ \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = \bigl(a_y b_z - a_z b_y,\; a_z b_x - a_x b_z,\; a_x b_y - a_y b_x\bigr). $$

Plug in the values:

$$\text{For }\hat{i}\text{-component: } 1\cdot b_3 - (-1)\cdot b_2 = b_3 + b_2,$$

$$\text{For }\hat{j}\text{-component: } (-1)\cdot b_1 - 0\cdot b_3 = -b_1,$$

$$\text{For }\hat{k}\text{-component: } 0\cdot b_2 - 1\cdot b_1 = -b_1.$$

Thus,

$$ \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = \bigl(b_3+b_2,\; -b_1,\; -b_1\bigr). $$

Step 2. Use the first given equation:

We have:

$$ \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = -\mathbf{c}. $$

Since

$$ -\mathbf{c} = (-1, 1, 1), $$

equate the corresponding components:

$$b_3 + b_2 = -1,$$

$$-b_1 = 1,$$

$$-b_1 = 1.$$

From the second equation, it follows immediately that:

$$ b_1 = -1. $$

Step 3. Use the second given equation:

We are given:

$$ \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 3. $$

Since $$\mathbf{a}=(0, 1, -1)$$ and $$\mathbf{b}=(b_1, b_2, b_3),$$ the dot product is:

$$ \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 0\cdot b_1 + 1\cdot b_2 + (-1)\cdot b_3 = b_2 - b_3. $$

Thus,

$$ b_2 - b_3 = 3. $$

Step 4. Solve the system of equations for $$b_2$$ and $$b_3$$:

We have:

$$b_2 + b_3 = -1,$$

$$b_2 - b_3 = 3.$$

Add the two equations:

$$ (b_2 + b_3) + (b_2 - b_3) = -1 + 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2b_2 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_2 = 1. $$

Substitute $$b_2 = 1$$ into $$b_2 + b_3 = -1$$:

$$ 1 + b_3 = -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_3 = -2. $$

Step 5. Write the vector $$\mathbf{b}$$:

We found:

$$b_1 = -1,$$

$$b_2 = 1,$$

$$b_3 = -2.$$

Thus,

$$ \mathbf{b} = -\hat{i} + \hat{j} - 2\hat{k}. $$

This corresponds to Option A.

content: $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$, then $a$ vector of magnitude 6 units, which is parallel to the vector $2 \bar{a}-\bar{b}+3 c$, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & 2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{c}} \\ & =(2-4+3) \hat{i}+(2+2-6) \hat{j}+(2-3+3) \hat{k} \\ & =\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Required vector is the multiple of the above vector and has magnitude 6 units.

Option (A) satisfies this condition.

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \bar{b}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\bar{c}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}$ such that $\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{a}}$ is perpendicular to $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$, then $\lambda$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { According to the given condition, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & (\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow[(-1+2 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(2+2 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(1+3 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}}] \cdot(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3(-1+2 \lambda)+(2+2 \lambda)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-3+6 \lambda+2+2 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{8} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\quad \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=x \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+(1-x) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \quad$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}=y \hat{\mathrm{i}}+x \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(1+x-y) \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ then $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})$ depends on
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ x & 1 & 1-x \\ y & x & 1+x-y \end{array}\right|\\ &\text { Applying } C_3 \Rightarrow C_3+C_1 \text {, we get }\\ &[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ x & 1 & 1 \\ y & x & 1+x \end{array}\right|=1(1+x-x)=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\bar{a}, \bar{b}$ and $\bar{c}$ be three vectors having magnitudes 1,1 and 2 respectively. If $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+\overline{\mathrm{b}}=\overline{0}$, then the acute angle between $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given, }|\vec{a}|=1,|\vec{b}|=1 \text { and } \mid \vec{c}=2\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { Also, } \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+\overline{\mathrm{b}}=\overline{0} \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}=\overline{0} \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}=\overline{0} \quad \quad \ldots\left[\because \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2=1\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}=-\overline{\mathrm{b}} \\ & \Rightarrow|(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=|-\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \\ & \Rightarrow|(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow|(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2-2\{(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}\}=|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2 \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}})^2|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2-2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}})(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}})=|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2 \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}})^2\left\{|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2-2\right\}+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2 \end{aligned}$$

$\Rightarrow-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}})^2+4=1 \quad \ldots\left[\because|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|^2=1,|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=4\right]$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}})^2=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}= \pm \sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{a}}| \overline{\mathrm{c}} \mid \cos \theta=\sqrt{3}, \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { where } \theta \text { is an acute angle between } \overline{\mathrm{a}} \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{c}}\\ &\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ are unit vectors inclined at $\frac{\pi}{3}$ with each other and $(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})) \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=5$, then the value of $5[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} |\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}| & =1 \\ \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}} & =|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \cos \frac{\pi}{3} \\ & =1 \times 1 \times \frac{1}{2} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, }[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})] \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=5 \quad\text{.... [Given]}\\ & \Rightarrow[(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}}](\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=5 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left[\frac{1}{2} \overline{\mathrm{~b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}}\right] \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=5 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=5 \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=10 \\ & \Rightarrow[\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=10 \\ & \Rightarrow-[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=10 \\ & \Rightarrow[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=-10 \\ & \Rightarrow 5[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=-50 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=2,|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|=3$ and $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}$ are mutually perpendicular vectors, then the area of the triangle whose vertices are $0, a+2 b, a-2 b$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let position vectors of } \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{~B}, \mathrm{C} \text { be }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 0, a+2 b, a-2 b \\ & \text { Area of } \left.\triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2}| | \overline{A B} \times \overline{A C} \right\rvert\, \\ & =\frac{1}{2}|(\bar{a}+2 \bar{b})(\bar{a}-2 \bar{b})| \\ & =\frac{1}{2}|\bar{a} \times \bar{a}-\bar{a} \times 2 \bar{b}+2 \bar{b} \times \bar{a}-2 \bar{b} \times \bar{b}| \\ & =\frac{1}{2}|2 \bar{b} \times \bar{a}+2 \bar{b} \times \bar{a}| \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 4|\bar{b} \times \bar{a}| \\ & =2 \times 2 \times 3 \\ & =12 \text { sq. units. } \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\bar{A}, \bar{B}, \bar{C}$ be vectors of lengths 3 units, 4 units, 5 units respectively. let $\bar{A}$ be perpendicular to $\overline{\mathrm{B}}+\overline{\mathrm{C}}, \overline{\mathrm{B}}$ be perpendicular to $\overline{\mathrm{C}}+\overline{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{C}}$ be perpendicular to $\bar{A}+\bar{B}$, then the length of vector $\overline{\mathrm{A}}+\overline{\mathrm{B}}+\overline{\mathrm{C}}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & |\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=3,|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}|=4 \text { and }|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=5 \\ & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})=0, \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}})=0 \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}})=0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Now, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & |\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=9+16+25+0 \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) } \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=50 \\ & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=5 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\bar{a}, \bar{b}$ and $\bar{c}$ be three non-zero vectors such that no two of them are collinear and $(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \overline{\mathrm{a}}$. If $\theta$ is the angle between vectors $\bar{b}$ and $\bar{c}$, then the value of $\operatorname{cosec} \theta$ is
explanation:
Given: $(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{~b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \overline{\mathrm{a}}$

We know that,

$$(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}})^{\overline{\mathrm{a}}}$$

On comparing, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{3}|\bar{b}||\bar{c}|=-\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}|\bar{b}||\vec{c}|=-|\bar{b}||\bar{c}| \cos \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{1}{9} \\ & \sin ^2 \theta=1-\cos ^2 \theta \\ & =1-\frac{1}{9} \\ & \therefore \quad \sin ^2 \theta=\frac{8}{9} \\ & \therefore \quad \sin \theta=\sqrt{\frac{8}{9}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3} \\ & \operatorname{cosec} \theta=\frac{3}{2 \sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\quad \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \quad \overline{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\beta \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \quad$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$, where $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$, be three vectors. If the projection at $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ on $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is $\frac{10}{3}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=-6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$, then the value of $\alpha^2+\beta^2-\alpha \beta$ is equal to
explanation:
Projection of $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ on $\bar{c}$ is $\frac{10}{3}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{\bar{a} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}}{\mid \bar{c}}=\frac{10}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{(\alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}=\frac{10}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha+3(2)-1(-2)}{\sqrt{9}}=\frac{10}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha+8}{3}=\frac{10}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=2 \\ & \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=-6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{cc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ 3 & -\beta \\ 1 & 4 \\ 1 & -2 \end{array}\right|=-6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow(2 \beta-8) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(6+\beta) \hat{\mathrm{k}}=-6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow 6+\beta=7 \Rightarrow \beta=1 \\ & \alpha^2+\beta^2-\alpha \beta=4+1-2=3 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ be vectors of magnitude 2,3 and 4 respectively. If $\bar{a}$ is perpendicular to $(\bar{b}+\bar{c}), \bar{b}$ is perpendicular to $(\bar{c}+\bar{a})$ and $\bar{c}$ is perpendicular to $(\bar{a}+\bar{b})$, then the magnitude of $\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \perp(\overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}), \overline{\mathrm{b}} \perp(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{c}} \perp(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=0, \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=0, \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} |\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}|^2 & =|\bar{a}|^2+|\bar{b}|^2+|\bar{c}|^2+2(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}+\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c}+\bar{c} \cdot \bar{a}) \\ & =2^2+3^2+4^2+2(0) \\ & =4+9+16 \\ \therefore\quad|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}|^2 & =29 \\ \Rightarrow|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}| & =\sqrt{29} \end{aligned}$$

content: The vector $\bar{a}=\alpha \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\beta \hat{k}$ lies in the plane of the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and bisects the angle between $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$. Then which one of the following gives possible values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ ?
explanation:
Since $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ bisects the angle between $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$.

$\therefore \quad$ The equation of bisector of $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{b}}+\hat{\mathrm{c}}) \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\beta \hat{\mathrm{k}}=\lambda\left(\frac{\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\beta \hat{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{2}}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})\\ &\text { On comparing, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \alpha=\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{2}}, 2=\sqrt{2 \lambda} \text { and } \beta=\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=1, \beta=1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: A unit vector coplanar with $\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and perpendicular to $\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ is
explanation:
Let $\bar{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \bar{b}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \bar{c}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$.

Then, required unit vectors are given by

$$\bar{\alpha}= \pm \frac{\bar{a} \times(\bar{b} \times \bar{c})}{|\bar{a} \times(\bar{b} \times \bar{c})|}$$

Now, $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}}$

$=2(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})-1(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=\hat{j}+\hat{k}$

$\therefore \quad|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})|=\sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2}$

Hence, required unit vectors are

$$\bar{\alpha}= \pm \frac{\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

content: Let $\bar{a}=3 \hat{i}-\alpha \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\bar{b}=\hat{i}+\alpha \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$. If the area of the parallelogram whose adjacent sides are represented by the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{b}}$, is $8 \sqrt{3}$ sq. units, then $\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 3 & -\alpha & 1 \\ 1 & \alpha & 3 \end{array}\right| \\ &=-4 \alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}-8 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \alpha \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \text { Area of parallelogram }=|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}| \end{aligned} \\ & \Rightarrow 8 \sqrt{3}=\sqrt{16 \alpha^2+64+16 \alpha^2} \\ & \Rightarrow 8 \sqrt{3}=\sqrt{32 \alpha^2+64} \end{aligned}$$

Squaring on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 192=32 \alpha^2+64 \\ & \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 32 \alpha^2=128 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha^2=4 \\ & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~b}}=(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\alpha \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\alpha \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ &=3-\alpha^2+3 \\ &=6-4 \\ &=2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\hat{a}$ and $\hat{b}$ be two unit vectors. If the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{a}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{~b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{d}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{a}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{~b}}$ are perpendicular to each other, then the angle between $\hat{a}$ and $\hat{b}$ is
explanation:
Let $\theta$ be the angle between $\hat{a}$ and $\hat{b}$.

Since $\bar{c}=\hat{a}+2 \hat{b}$ and $\bar{d}=5 \hat{a}+4 \hat{b} \quad$ are perpendicular to each other.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~d}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\hat{\mathrm{a}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{~b}}) \cdot(5 \hat{\mathrm{a}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{~b}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5(\hat{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{a}})+14(\hat{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{~b}})+8(\hat{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{~b}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5|\hat{\mathrm{a}}|^2+\left.14|\hat{\mathrm{a}}| \hat{\mathrm{b}}|\cos \theta+8| \hat{\mathrm{b}}\right|^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5+14 \cos \theta+8=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=-\frac{13}{14} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{13}{14}\right) \end{aligned} $$

content: If the vectors $a \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \hat{i}+\hat{j}+c \hat{k}$ $(a \neq b, c \neq 1)$ are coplanar, then $\frac{1}{1-a}+\frac{1}{1-b}+\frac{1}{1-c}$ has the value __________.
explanation:
Since $\left|\begin{array}{lll}a & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & b & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & c\end{array}\right|=0$ Applying $\mathrm{R}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_2-\mathrm{R}_1$ and $\mathrm{R}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_3-\mathrm{R}_1$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} a & 1 & 1 \\ 1-a & b-1 & 0 \\ 1-a & 0 & c-1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a(b-1)(c-1)-(1-a)(c-1)-(1-a)(b-1)=0 \end{aligned}$$

Dividing by $(1-a)(1-b)(1-c)$,

we get $\frac{a}{1-a}+\frac{1}{1-b}+\frac{1}{1-c}=0\quad\text{.... (i)}$

Consider, $\frac{1}{1-\mathrm{a}}+\frac{1}{1-\mathrm{b}}+\frac{1}{1-\mathrm{c}}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{1-a}-\frac{a}{1-a} \quad\text{.... [From (i)]}\\ & =1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ are three non-coplanar vectors, then $(\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}) \cdot[(\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \times(\bar{a}+\bar{c})]$ equals
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { (D) Std.12|Part-1|Ch-5| Exercise-5.5 } \\ & {[\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}] \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})]} \\ & =(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})]+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ & =0+[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}] \\ & =[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}]+[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}] \\ & =[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{a}}]+[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]+[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{a}}]+[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}] \\ & =0+0+[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{a}}]+0 \\ & =-[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{~b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}] \end{aligned}$$

content: Suppose that $\bar{p}, \bar{q}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{r}}$ are three non-coplanar vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$.
