content: Two adjacent sides of a parallelogram $$\mathrm{ABCD}$$ are given by $$\overline{A B}=2 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{AD}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$. The side $$\mathrm{AD}$$ is rotated by an acute angle $$\alpha$$ in the plane of parallelogram so that $$\mathrm{AD}$$ becomes $$\mathrm{AD}^{\prime}$$. If $$\mathrm{AD}^{\prime}$$ makes a right angle with side AB, then the cosine of the angle $$\alpha$$ is given by
explanation:
Let $$\theta$$ be the angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{AD}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \cos \theta & =\frac{\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{AD}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}||\overline{\mathrm{AD}}|} \\ & =\frac{(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+11 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{4+100+121} \sqrt{1+4+4}} \\ & =\frac{-2+20+22}{\sqrt{225} \sqrt{9}} \\ & =\frac{40}{45} \\ & =\frac{8}{9} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{8}{9}\right)^2}=\frac{\sqrt{17}}{9}$$

$$\alpha$$ is the angle of rotation of AD.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The angle between side $$\mathrm{AB}$$ and $$\mathrm{AD}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\alpha+\theta \\ & =90^{\circ} \quad ....[\text{Given}] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \cos (\alpha+\theta)=\cos \left(90^{\circ}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \cos \alpha \cos \theta-\sin \alpha \sin \theta=0 \\ & \therefore \quad 8 \cos \alpha=\sqrt{17} \sin \alpha \\ & \therefore \quad 64 \cos ^2 \alpha=17\left(1-\cos ^2 \alpha\right) \\ & \therefore \quad 81 \cos ^2 \alpha=17 \\ & \therefore \quad \cos \alpha=\frac{\sqrt{17}}{9} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the area of the triangle with vertices $$(1,2,0)$$, $$(1,0,2)$$ and $$(0, x, 1)$$ is $$\sqrt{6}$$ square units, then the value of $x$ is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{A} \equiv(1,2,0), \mathrm{B} \equiv(1,0,2)$$ and $$\mathrm{C} \equiv(0, x, 1)$$

$$\therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+(x-2) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Area of $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}=\frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}|=\sqrt{6}$$

$$\begin{aligned} |\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}| & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 0 & -2 & 2 \\ -1 & x-2 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{\mathrm{i}}[-2-2(x-2)]-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(0+2)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(0-2) \\ & =(2-2 x) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{2}|\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overline{\mathrm{AC}}|=\sqrt{6} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(2-2 x)^2+4+4}=\sqrt{6} \\ & \Rightarrow(2-2 x)^2=16 \\ & \Rightarrow 4-8 x+4 x^2=16 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-2 x-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-3)(x+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=3 \text { or }-1 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\overline{\mathrm{A}}$$ be a vector parallel to line of intersection of planes $$P_1$$ and $$P_2$$ through origin. $$P_1$$ is parallel to the vectors $$2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$ and $$4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$ and $$P_2$$ is parallel to $$\hat{j}-\hat{k}$$ and $$3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$$, then the angle between $$\bar{A}$$ and $$2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$$ is
explanation:
Vector equation of the plane passing through the point $$A(\bar{a})$$ and parallel to non-zero vectors $$\bar{b}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ is $$\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})$$

Plane $$P_1$$ is passing through the origin and parallel to vectors $$\overline{b_1}=2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$ and $$\overline{c_1}=4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$

$$\therefore \quad \overline{b_1} \times \overline{c_1}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 4 & -3 \end{array}\right|=-18 \hat{i}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of $$P_1$$ is: $$r \cdot(-18 \mathrm{i})=0$$

Plane $$P_2$$ is passing through the origin and parallel to vectors $$\overline{b_2}=\hat{j}-\hat{k}$$ and $$\overline{c_2}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$$

$$\therefore \quad \overline{b_2} \times \overline{c_2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 3 & 3 & 0 \end{array}\right|=3 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of $$P_2$$ is : $$r_2 \cdot(3 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})=0$$

Note that $$\overline{\mathrm{A}}$$ is parallel to the cross product of $$-18 \hat{i}$$ and $$3 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$

$$\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -18 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & -3 & -3 \end{array}\right|=-54 \hat{j}+54 \hat{k}$$

Let $$\theta$$ be the required angle.

$$\therefore \quad \theta=$$ Angle between $$54(-\hat{j}+\hat{k})$$ and $$2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \cos \theta & =\frac{54 \times(-1-2)}{54 \sqrt{0+1+1} \sqrt{4+1+4}} \\ & = \pm \frac{3}{3 \sqrt{2}} \\ & = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \therefore \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{4} & , \frac{3 \pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\overline{\mathrm{u}}, \overline{\mathrm{v}}, \overline{\mathrm{w}}$$ are three vectors such that $$|\overline{\mathrm{u}}|=1, |\bar{v}|=2,|\bar{w}|=3$$. If the projection of $$\bar{v}$$ along $$\bar{u}$$ is equal to projection of $$\bar{w}$$ along $$\bar{u}$$ and $$\bar{v}, \bar{w}$$ are perpendicular to each other, then $$|\bar{u}-\bar{v}+\bar{w}|=$$
explanation:
$$|\overline{\mathrm{u}}|=1,|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}|=2,|\overline{\mathrm{w}}|=3$$

According to the given condition, (Projection of $$\overline{\mathrm{v}}$$ along $$\overline{\mathrm{u}}$$ ) $$=(\text { Projection of } \bar{w} \text { along } \bar{u})$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{\overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{u}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{u}}|}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{w}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{u}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{u}}|} \\ & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{u}}=\overline{\mathrm{w}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{u}} \\ & \therefore \quad(\overline{\mathrm{w}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{u}}=0 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \end{aligned}$$

Now consider, $$|\overline{\mathrm{u}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}}+\overline{\mathrm{w}}|=\sqrt{|\overline{\mathrm{u}}+\overline{\mathrm{w}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}}|^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{|\overline{\mathrm{u}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{w}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}}|^2+2 \overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{w}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}})} \\ & =\sqrt{(1)^2+|\overline{\mathrm{w}}-\overline{\mathrm{v}}|^2+0} \quad \text{.... [From (i)]}\\ & =\sqrt{1+|\overline{\mathrm{w}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{v}}|^2-2(\overline{\mathrm{w}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{v}})} \\ & =\sqrt{1+9+4+0} \quad ...[\because \overline{w} ~\text{and}~\overline{v}~\text{are perpendicular}]\\ & =\sqrt{14} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \bar{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \bar{c}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$$, then a vector $$\overline{\mathrm{d}}$$ which is parallel to vector $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ and which $$\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{d}}=15$$, is
explanation:
Here, $$\bar{c}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$$

And given that $$\bar{c} \cdot \bar{d}=15$$

We verify given options one by one to satisfy the above condition.

Consider option (B),

For $$\overline{\mathrm{d}}=90 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-42 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{c} \cdot \bar{d} & =(2)(90)+(-1)(-3)+(4)(-42) \\ & =180+3-168=15 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Option (B) is correct.

content: The unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors $$\bar{a}+\bar{b}$$ and $$\bar{a}-\bar{b}$$, where $$\bar{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \bar{a}+\bar{b} & =(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})+(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) \\ & =4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+6 \hat{k} \\ \bar{a}-\bar{b} & =(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) \\ & =-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Vector perpendicular to $$(\bar{a}+\bar{b})$$ and $$(\bar{a}-\bar{b})$$ is

$$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -1 & 6 \\ -2 & 3 & -4\end{array}\right|=-14 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Required unit vector is

$$\frac{-14 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{(-14)^2+4^2+(10)^2}}=\frac{-14 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{312}}$$

content: Let $$\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, \bar{b}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}$$ and $$\bar{c}$$ be a vector such that $$|\bar{c}-\bar{a}|=4,|(\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \times \bar{c}|=3$$ and the angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ is $$\frac{\pi}{6}$$, then $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \overline{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ \therefore \quad & |\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=\sqrt{4+1+4}=3 \\ & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad & |\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|=\sqrt{4+4+1}=3 \end{array}$$

Angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ is $$\frac{\pi}{6}$$ .... [Given]

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{|(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}|}{|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}|} \\ & \frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{3 \times|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|} \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=2 \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$|\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=4$$ ..... [Given]

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow|\bar{c}|^2+|\bar{a}|^2-2 \bar{a} \cdot \bar{c}=16 \\ & \Rightarrow 4+9-2 a \cdot c=16 \\ & \Rightarrow a \cdot c=\frac{-3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the area of the parallelogram with $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$ as two adjacent sides is $$16 \mathrm{sq}$$. units, then the area of the parallelogram having $$3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ as two adjacent sides (in sq. units) is
explanation:
Area of the parallelogram with $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$ as two adjacent sides is $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|$$

$$\therefore \quad|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|=16$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Area of the required parallelogram

$$\begin{aligned} & =|(3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{b}})| \\ & =|3(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+9(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})+2(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})| \\ & =0+9|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|-2|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|+0 \\ & =7|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}| \\ & =7 \times 16 \\ & =112 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}$$ are such that $$\bar{a}+\lambda \bar{b}$$ is perpendicular to $$\bar{c}$$, then the value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
According to the given condition, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & (\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=0 \\ \therefore \quad & {[(2+2 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(3+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2-\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}}] \cdot(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}})=0} \\ \therefore \quad & 3(2+2 \lambda)-(3+\lambda)=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 6+6 \lambda-3-\lambda=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 3+5 \lambda=0 \\ \therefore & \lambda=\frac{-3}{5} \end{array}$$

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\alpha \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\beta \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ are linearly dependent vectors and $$|\bar{c}|=\sqrt{3}$$, then the values of $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ are respectively.
explanation:
Note that only for option (A), i.e., for $$\alpha=1$$ and $$\beta=1,|\vec{c}|=\sqrt{3}$$ holds true.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Option (A) is correct.

content: If the volume of the parallelopiped is $$158 \mathrm{~cu}$$. units whose coterminus edges are given by the vectors $$\bar{a}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+n \hat{k}), \bar{b}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-n \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{c}=\hat{i}+n \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$, where $$n \geq 0$$, then the value of $$n$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Volume of parallelopiped }=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & n \\ 2 & 4 & -n \\ 1 & n & 3 \end{array}\right|=158 \\ & \Rightarrow 1\left(12+n^2\right)-1(6+n)+n(2 n-4)=158 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 n^2-5 n-152=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(3 n+19)(n-8)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow n=8 \quad \ldots[\because n \geq 0] \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}, \bar{b}, \bar{c}$$ are three vectors such that $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}})+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}})=0 \quad$$ and $$\quad|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=1$$, $$|\bar{b}|=8$$ and $$|\bar{c}|=4$$, then $$|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}|$$ has the value _________.
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & |\bar{a}|=1,|\bar{b}|=8,|\bar{c}|=4 \text {, and } \\ & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}})+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}})=0 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} &|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}|^2=|\bar{a}|^2+|\bar{b}|^2+|\bar{c}|^2+2(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}+\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c}+\bar{c} \cdot \bar{a}) \\ & \Rightarrow|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}|^2=1+64+16+0 \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) ] } \\ & \Rightarrow|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}|^2=81 \\ & \Rightarrow|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}|=9 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{b}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}$$. If $$\bar{c}$$ is a vector such that $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|,|\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=2 \sqrt{2}$$ and the angle between $$(\bar{a} \times \bar{b})$$ and $$\bar{c}$$ is $$\frac{\pi}{6}$$, then $$|(\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \times \bar{c}|$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & |\bar{c}-\bar{a}|=2 \sqrt{2}, \\ & \Rightarrow|\bar{c}|^2+|\bar{a}|^2-2(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{c})=8 \\ & \Rightarrow|\bar{c}|^2+9-2|\bar{c}|=8 \quad \ldots[\because \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|]\\ & \Rightarrow(|\bar{c}|-1)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow|\bar{c}|=1 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} |(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}| & =|(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \sin \frac{\pi}{6} \\ & =|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|(1)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \quad \text{... [From (i)]}\\ & =\frac{3}{2} \quad \ldots[\because \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}] \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\quad \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}, \quad \overline{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}} \quad$$ and $$\quad \overline{\mathrm{r}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{r}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$, then the value $$\frac{\overline{\mathrm{r}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{r}}|}$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=x \hat{\mathrm{i}}+y \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ then

$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{r}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}} \quad \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{r}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}=\overline{0} \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ x & y-2 & \mathrm{z}+1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|=\overline{0} \\ & \Rightarrow(-\mathrm{z}-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(-\mathrm{z}-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(x-y+2) \hat{\mathrm{k}}=\overline{0} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{z}=-1, x-y=-2 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\overline{\mathrm{r}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=(\overline{\mathrm{r}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\overline{0}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ x-1 & y-1 & z \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|=\overline{0} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow(1-y-2 \mathrm{z}) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(1-x) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2 x-2) \hat{\mathrm{k}}=\overline{0} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 1-y-2 \mathrm{z}=0, x=1 \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x=1, y=3, z=-1 \\ & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{r}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & |\overline{\mathrm{r}}|=\sqrt{1+9+1}=\sqrt{11} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\overline{\mathrm{r}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{r}}|}=\frac{\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{11}} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\bar{a}, \bar{b}$$ and $$\bar{c}$$ be three unit vectors such that $$\bar{a} \times(\bar{b} \times \bar{c})=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(\bar{b}+\bar{c})$$. If $$\bar{b}$$ is not parallel to $$\bar{c}$$, then the angle between $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \\ & \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \overline{\mathrm{c}} \end{aligned}$$

On comparing, we get $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \quad \ldots[\because|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=1] \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{5 \pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ are two unit vectors such that $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ and $$5 \bar{a}-4 \bar{b}$$ are perpendicular to each other, then the angle between $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$ is
explanation:
Since $$\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}$$ and $$5 \bar{a}-4 \bar{b}$$ are perpendicular to each other

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & (\overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot(5 \overline{\mathrm{a}}-4 \overline{\mathrm{b}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2-8|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+6 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -3+6|\overline{\mathrm{a}}||\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta=0 \quad \ldots[\because|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=1] \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$(\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \times \bar{c}=-5 \bar{a}+4 \bar{b}$$ and $$\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}=3$$, then the value of $$\bar{a} \times(\bar{b} \times \bar{c})$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & (\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}} \\ & \text { But, }(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=-5 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+4 \overline{\mathrm{b}} \\ \therefore \quad & -5 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+4 \overline{\mathrm{b}}=(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}} \end{aligned}$$

Comparing, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=4 \\ & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \overline{\mathrm{c}} \\ & =4 \overline{\mathrm{b}}-3 \overline{\mathrm{c}} \quad \ldots[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}=3 \text { (given) }] \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{p}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{q}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$$. Then a vector of magnitude $$5 \sqrt{3}$$ units perpendicular to the vector $$\bar{q}$$ and coplanar with $$\bar{p}$$ and $$\bar{q}$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\bar{r}=a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+c \hat{k}$$

As $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}$$ is perpendicular to $$\overline{\mathrm{q}}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \bar{r} \cdot \bar{q}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a-2 b+c=0 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Also, $$\bar{r}$$ is coplanar with vectors $$\bar{p}$$ and $$\bar{q}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \bar{p} & \bar{q} & \bar{r} \end{array}\right]=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \\ a & b & c \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 a-3 c=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a-c=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a=c \quad \text{..... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} \quad \mathrm{b} & =\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{r}} & =\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Now, the magnitude of required vector is $$5 \sqrt{3}$$ units.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Required vector } & =5 \sqrt{3} \times \frac{\overline{\mathrm{r}}}{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}|} \\ & =5 \sqrt{3} \times \frac{\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{3}}=5(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$ are two unit vectors such that $$\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}$$ and $$5 \bar{a}-4 \bar{b}$$ are perpendicular to each other, then the angle between $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$ is
explanation:
Given that, $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ and $$5 \overline{\mathrm{a}}-4 \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ are perpendicular to each other.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad(\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}) \cdot(5 \bar{a}-4 \bar{b})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5|\bar{a}|^2-8|\bar{b}|^2-4 \bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}+10 \bar{b} \cdot \bar{a}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-3+6 \bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}=0 \quad ...[|\bar{a}|=|\bar{b}|=1]\\ & \Rightarrow 6|\bar{a}||\bar{b}| \cos \theta=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{m} \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\mathrm{nc}$$, where $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+13 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-18 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$, then $$\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=$$
explanation:
Given:

$$\begin{aligned} & \bar{a}=4 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}-18 \hat{k} \\ & \bar{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ & \bar{c}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

Also, $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{m} \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\mathrm{n} \overline{\mathrm{c}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 4 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}-18 \hat{k}=m(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+n(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}) \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}-18 \hat{k} \\ & \quad=(m+2 n) \hat{i}+(-2 m+3 n) \hat{j}+(3 m-4 n) \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

Comparing, we get

$$m+2 n=4 \text { and }-2 m+3 n=13$$

Solving above equations, we get $$\mathrm{m}=-2$ and $\mathrm{n}=3$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=-2+3=1 $$

content: If the volume of tetrahedron, whose vertices are with position vectors $$\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k},-\hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, 5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\lambda \hat{k}$$ and $$7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}$$ is 11 cubic units, then value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \bar{a}=\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k} \text {, } \\ & \overline{\mathrm{b}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \text {, } \\ & \overline{\mathrm{c}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{k}} \text {, } \\ & \overline{\mathrm{d}}=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(\lambda-10) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{AD}}=6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Volume of tetrahedron }=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{AB}} & \overline{\mathrm{AC}} & \overline{\mathrm{AD}} \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow 11=\frac{1}{6}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & 3 & -3 \\ 4 & 5 & \lambda-10 \\ 6 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow 11=\frac{1}{6}\{-2(-15-2 \lambda+20)-3(-12-6 \lambda+60) -3(8-30)\}\\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=7 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: Scalar projection of the line segment joining the points $$\mathrm{A}(-2,0,3), \mathrm{B}(1,4,2)$$ on the line whose direction ratios are $$6,-2,3$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\bar{a}$$ be the vector joining $$\mathrm{A}(-2,0,3)$$ and $$\mathrm{B}(1,4,2)$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{a}}=(1-(-2)) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(4-0) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2-3) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & =3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \text { and } \bar{b}=6 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Projection }=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|}=\frac{3 \times 6+4 \times(-2)-1 \times 3}{\sqrt{6^2+(-2)^2+3^2}} \\ & =\frac{18-8-3}{\sqrt{49}} \\ & =\frac{7}{7} \\ & =1 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$$ are such that $$(\bar{a}+\lambda \bar{b})$$ is perpendicular to $$\bar{c}$$, then the value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\overline{\mathrm{d}}=\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \overline{\mathrm{d}} & =(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & =2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+2 \lambda \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\lambda \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & =(2 \lambda+2) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(3+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2-\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\overline{\mathrm{d}}$$ is perpendicular to $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \quad \bar{c} \cdot \bar{d}=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow(\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \cdot[(2 \lambda+2) \hat{i}+(3+\lambda) \hat{j}+(2-\lambda) \hat{k}]=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 1(2 \lambda+2)+3(3+\lambda)=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 2 \lambda+2+9+3 \lambda=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 5 \lambda+11=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-11}{5} \end{aligned}$$

content: The vector projection of $$\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$$ on $$\overline{\mathrm{CD}}$$, where $$A \equiv(2,-3,0), B \equiv(1,-4,-2), C \equiv(4,6,8)$$ and $$\mathrm{D} \equiv(7,0,10)$$, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{CD}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Vector projection of $$\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$$ on $$\overline{\mathrm{CD}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =(\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{CD}}) \frac{\overline{\mathrm{CD}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{CD}}|^2} \\ & =(-3+6-4) \frac{(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\left(\sqrt{3^2+(-6)^2+2^2}\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{-1}{49}(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \end{aligned}$$

content: The vectors are $$\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, \bar{b}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}$$. If $$\bar{c}$$ is a vector such that $$\bar{a} \cdot \bar{c}=|\bar{c}|$$ and $$|\bar{c}-\bar{a}|=2 \sqrt{2}$$, angle between $$\bar{a} \times \bar{b}$$ and $$\bar{c}$$ is $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$, then $$|(\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \times \bar{c}|$$ is
explanation:
Given that angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$ is $\frac{\pi}{4}$$

$$\therefore \quad|(\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \times \bar{c}|=|(\bar{a} \times \bar{b})||\bar{c}| \sin \frac{\pi}{4}$$ ..... (i)

Now, $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 & 0\end{array}\right|$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\hat{\mathrm{i}}(0+2)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(0+2)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(2-1) \\ & =2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \quad |\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|= & \sqrt{2^2+(-2)^2+1}=3 \end{aligned}$$

Given, $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

$$a=\sqrt{2^2+1^2+(-2)^2}=3$$

Given, $$|\bar{c}-\bar{a}|=2 \sqrt{2}$$

Squaring on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & |\bar{c}|^2+|\bar{a}|^2-2 \bar{a} \cdot \bar{c}=8 \\ & \Rightarrow|\bar{c}|^2+3^2-2|\bar{c}|=8 \quad ...[\because \bar{a} . \bar{c}=|\bar{c}|] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow|\bar{c}|^2-2|\bar{c}|+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(|\bar{c}|-1)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow|\bar{c}|=1 \end{aligned}$$

From (i),

$$\begin{aligned} |(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}| & =|(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{4} \\ & =3 \times 1 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & =\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \bar{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \bar{c}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}$$, then a vector in the plane of $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$, whose projection on $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ is $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$, is
explanation:
Let $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}$$ be the vector coplanar to $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$. Then,

$$\begin{aligned} \overline{\mathrm{r}} & =\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\mathrm{m} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \\ & =(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mathrm{m}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & =\hat{\mathrm{i}}(1+\mathrm{m})+\hat{\mathrm{j}}(2-\mathrm{m})+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(1+\mathrm{m}) \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Since the projection of $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}$$ along $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ is $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{\overline{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|}= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{(1+\mathrm{m})+(2-\mathrm{m})-(1+\mathrm{m})}{\sqrt{3}}= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \Rightarrow(1+\mathrm{m})+(2-\mathrm{m})-(1+\mathrm{m})= \pm 1 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{m}=3 \text { or } \mathrm{m}=1 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $$\mathrm{m}=3$$ in equation (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \bar{r}=\hat{i}(1+3)+\hat{j}(2-3)+\hat{k}(1+3) \\ & \Rightarrow \bar{r}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\bar{a}, \bar{b}, \bar{c}$$ be three non-zero vectors, such that no two of them are collinear and $$(\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \times \bar{c}=\frac{1}{3}|\bar{b}||\bar{c}| \bar{a}$$. If $$\theta$$ is the angle between the vectors $$\bar{b}$$ and $$\bar{c}$$, then the value of $$\sin \theta$$ is
explanation:
Given: $$(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{b}}||\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}$$

We know that,

$$(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{b}}-(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \overline{\mathrm{a}}$$

On comparing, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{b}}||\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=-\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{3}|\overline{\mathrm{b}}||\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=-|\overline{\mathrm{b}}||\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}| \cos \theta \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \theta=\frac{-1}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{1}{9} \\ & \sin ^2 \theta=1-\cos ^2 \theta \\ & =1-\frac{1}{9} \\ \therefore \quad & \sin ^2 \theta=\frac{8}{9} \\ \therefore \quad & \sin \theta=\sqrt{\frac{8}{9}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$, then vector $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}$$ satisfying $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{r}}=\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{r}}=3$$ is
explanation:
Given $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{r}}=3$$

$$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{r}}=\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$

Let $$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=x \hat{\mathrm{i}}+y \hat{\mathrm{j}}+z \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{r}} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ x & y & \mathrm{z} \end{array}\right| \\ & =(\mathrm{z}-y) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(\mathrm{z}-x)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(y-x) \end{aligned}$$

Given $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{r}}=\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$

$$\therefore \quad(\mathrm{z}-y) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(\mathrm{z}-x) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(y-x) \hat{\mathrm{k}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$

Comparing

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{z}-y=0 \quad \text{..... (i)}\\ & \mathrm{z}-x=-1 \quad \text{..... (ii)}\\ & y-x=-1 \quad \text{..... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Also, $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{r}}=3$$

$$\begin{aligned} & (\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})(x \hat{\mathrm{i}}+y \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{zk})=3 \\ & x+y+z=3 \quad \text{..... (iv)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving equations (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x=\frac{5}{3}, y=\frac{2}{3}, \mathrm{z}=\frac{2}{3} \\ \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{r}} & =\frac{5}{3} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{2}{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\frac{2}{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The magnitude of the projection of the vector $$2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ on the vector perpendicular to the plane containing the vectors $$\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ and $$\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ is
explanation:
The vector perpendicular to both vectors containing $$(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$$ and $$(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & =(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \times(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the magnitude of the projection of vector $$(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$$ on $$(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left|\frac{(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})}{\sqrt{1^2+(-2)^2+(1)^2}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{2-2+1}{\sqrt{6}}\right| \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}, \bar{b}$$ and $$\bar{c}$$ are any three non-zero vectors, then $$(\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}+\bar{c}) \cdot[(\bar{a}-\bar{b}) \times(\bar{a}-\bar{b}-\bar{c})]=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & (\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}+\bar{c}) \cdot[(\bar{a}-\bar{b}) \times(\bar{a}-\bar{b}-\bar{c})] \\ & =(\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}+\bar{c}) \cdot(\bar{a} \times \bar{a}-\bar{a} \times \bar{b}-\bar{a} \times \bar{c}-\bar{b} \times \bar{a}+\bar{b} \times \bar{b}+\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) \\ & =(\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}+\bar{c}) \cdot(\overline{0}-\bar{a} \times \bar{b}-\bar{a} \times \bar{c}+\bar{a} \times \bar{b}+\overline{0}+\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) \\ & =(\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}+\bar{c}) \cdot(\bar{c} \times \bar{a}+\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) \\ & =\bar{a} \cdot(\bar{c} \times \bar{a})+\bar{a} \cdot(\bar{b} \times \bar{c})+2 \bar{b} \cdot(\bar{c} \times \bar{a})+2 \bar{b} \cdot(\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) \\ & +\bar{c} \cdot(\bar{c} \times \bar{a})+\bar{c} \cdot(\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\overline{0}+\bar{a} \cdot(\bar{b} \times \bar{c})+2 \bar{b} \cdot(\bar{c} \times \bar{a})+2 \times 0-0-0 \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right]+2\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{b} & \bar{c} & \bar{a} \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right]+2\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right] \\ & =3\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: Vectors $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ are such that $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=1 ;|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=4$$ and $$\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}=2$$. If $$\bar{c}=2 \bar{a} \times \bar{b}-3 \bar{b}$$, then the angle between $$\bar{b}$$ and $$\bar{c}$$ is
explanation:
Given: $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=1,|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=4$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}=2, \overline{\mathrm{c}}=2 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}-3 \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$

Now that, $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2=|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2-|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2$$

$$\therefore \quad|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2=16-4=12$$

Given that $$\bar{c}=2 \bar{a} \times \bar{b}-3 \bar{b}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & |\bar{c}|^2=(2 \bar{a} \times \bar{b}-3 \bar{b})^2 \\ & |\bar{c}|^2=4|\bar{a} \times \bar{b}|^2+9|\bar{b}|^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & |\bar{c}|^2=4(12)+9(16) \\ & |\bar{c}|^2=192 \\ & |\bar{c}|=8 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c}=b \cdot(2 \bar{a} \times \bar{b}-3 \bar{b})$$

$$\therefore \quad \bar{b} \cdot \bar{c}=-3|\bar{b}|^2=-48$$

Angle between $$b$$ and $$c$$ is given by

$$\begin{aligned} & \cos \theta=\frac{(\bar{b} \cdot \bar{c})}{(|b| \| c \mid)}=\frac{-48}{(4 \times 8 \sqrt{3})} \\ & \cos \theta=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\ & \theta=\frac{5 \pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: Two adjacent sides of a parallelogram are $$2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}$$ and $$\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$, then the unit vector parallel to its diagonal is
explanation:
Let $$\vec{a}$$ and $$\vec{b}$$ be the adjacent sides of a parallelogram, where

$$\begin{aligned} & \vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k} \\ & \vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

Let diagonal be $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \vec{c} & =\vec{a}+\vec{b} \\ \vec{c} & =2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}+\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \\ & =3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { Magnitude of } \begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} & =\sqrt{3^2+(-6)^2+(2)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{49}=7 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Unit vector in direction of diagonal $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\vec{c}}{|\vec{c}|} \\ & =\frac{1}{7}(3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \\ & =\frac{3}{7} \hat{i}-\frac{6}{7} \hat{j}+\frac{2}{7} \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{E}$$ and $$\mathrm{F}$$ are the mid-points of the sides $$\mathrm{BC}$$, $$\mathrm{CA}$$ and $$\mathrm{AB}$$ of triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$ respectively, then $$\overline{\mathrm{AD}}+\frac{2}{3} \overline{\mathrm{BE}}+\frac{1}{3} \overline{\mathrm{CF}}=$$
explanation:
Let the position vector of A, B, C, D, E, F be $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}, \overline{\mathrm{d}}, \overline{\mathrm{e}}, \overline{\mathrm{f}}$$ respectively.

$$\therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}}{2}, \overline{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}}}{2}, \overline{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}}{2}$$

Now, $$\overline{\mathrm{AD}}+\frac{2}{3} \overline{\mathrm{BE}}+\frac{1}{3} \overline{\mathrm{CF}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\overline{\mathrm{d}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{2}{3}(\overline{\mathrm{e}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}})+\frac{1}{3}(\overline{\mathrm{f}}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \\ & =\frac{\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}}{2}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}}}{2}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}\right)+\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}}{2}-\overline{\mathrm{c}}\right) \\ & =\frac{\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}}{2}+\frac{\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{b}}}{3}+\frac{\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{c}}}{6} \\ & =\frac{3 \overline{\mathrm{c}}-3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}}{6} \\ & =\frac{3}{6}(\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \overline{\mathrm{AC}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If two vertices of a triangle are $$\mathrm{A}(3,1,4)$$ and $$\mathrm{B}(-4,5,-3)$$ and the centroid of the triangle is $$G(-1,2,1)$$, then the third vertex $$C$$ of the triangle is
explanation:
Let $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{g}}$$ be the position vectors of $$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$$ and $$\mathrm{G}$$ respectively.

$$\begin{aligned} & \bar{a}=3 \hat{i}+1 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, \\ & \bar{b}=-4 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}, \\ & \bar{g}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \end{aligned}$$

$$\mathrm{G}$$ is centroid of $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \bar{g} & =\frac{\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}}{3} \\ 3 \bar{g} & =\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c} \\ & 3(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}-4 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}+\bar{c} \\ \therefore \quad \bar{c} & =-3 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}-3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+4 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ & =-2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Third vertex $$C \equiv(-2,0,2)$$

content: Let two non-collinear vectors $$\hat{a}$$ and $$\hat{b}$$ form an acute angle. A point $$\mathrm{P}$$ moves, so that at any time $$t$$ the position vector $$\overline{\mathrm{OP}}$$, where $$\mathrm{O}$$ is origin, is given by $$\hat{a} \sin t+\hat{b} \cos t$$, when $$P$$ is farthest from origin $$O$$, let $$M$$ be the length of $$\overline{\mathrm{OP}}$$ and $$\hat{\mathrm{u}}$$ be the unit vector along $$\overline{\mathrm{OP}}$$, then
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{M}=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}| \\ & M=\sqrt{(\hat{a} \sin t+\hat{b} \cos t)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(\hat{a} \sin t)^2+(\hat{b} \cos t)^2+2(\hat{a} \sin t) \cdot(\hat{b} \cos t)} \\ & =\sqrt{\sin ^2 \mathrm{t}+\cos ^2 \mathrm{t}+\hat{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{b}}(2 \sin \mathrm{t} \cos \mathrm{t})} \\ & =\sqrt{1+\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}(\sin 2 t)} \\ & \text { Maximum value of } \sin 2 t=1 \\ & \therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{t}=\sin ^{-1}(1) \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{M}=\sqrt{1+\hat{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{b}}(\mathrm{l})} \\ & =(1+\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b})^{\frac{1}{2}} \\ & \text { Now, } \hat{u}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{OP}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{OP}}|} \\ & =\frac{\hat{a} \sin t+\hat{b} \cos t}{|\hat{a} \sin t+\hat{b} \cos t|} \\ & =\frac{\hat{a}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\hat{b}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}{\left|\hat{a}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\hat{b}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right|} \\ \end{aligned}$$

Unit vector of OP is

$$\hat{u}=\frac{\hat{a}+\hat{b}}{|(\hat{a}+\hat{b})|} $$

content: The distance of the point having position vector $$\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}$$, from the straight line passing through the point $$(2,-3,-4)$$ and parallel to the vector $$6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}$$ is units.
explanation:
Given equation of the line is

$$\begin{aligned} \overline{\mathrm{r}} & =\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}} \\ \therefore \quad \overline{\mathrm{r}} & =2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\lambda(6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \end{aligned}$$

To find: Its distance from point $$\bar{\alpha}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { Required distance }=\sqrt{|\bar{\alpha}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2-\left[\frac{(\bar{\alpha}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|}\right]^2} \\ & \text { Here, }|\bar{\alpha}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2=6 \text { and }\left[\frac{(\bar{\alpha}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|}\right]^2=\frac{25}{61} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Required distance }=\sqrt{\frac{341}{61}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The scalar product of the vector $$\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ with a unit vector along the sum of the vectors $$2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}$$ and $$\lambda \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$ is equal to 1 , then value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
$$(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+(\lambda \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} $$

Unit vector along the above vector is

$$\frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{(2+\lambda)^2+6^2+(-2)^2}}=\frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{\lambda^2+4 \lambda+44}}$$

Scalar product of $$(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$$ with this unit vector is 1 .

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & (\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot \frac{(2+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{\lambda^2+4 \lambda+44}}=1 \\ & \frac{(2+\lambda)+6-2}{\sqrt{\lambda^2+4 \lambda+44}}=1 \\ & \sqrt{\lambda^2+4 \lambda+44}=\lambda+6 \\ \therefore \quad & \lambda^2+4 \lambda+44=(\lambda+6)^2 \\ & 8 \lambda=8 \\ & \lambda=1 \end{array}$$

content: If $$[(\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}+3 \bar{c}) \times(\bar{b}+2 \bar{c}+3 \bar{a})] \cdot(\bar{c}+2 \bar{a}+3 \bar{b})=54$$ then the value of $$\left[\begin{array}{lll}\bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c}\end{array}\right]$$ is
explanation:
R.H.S. of the given equality can be written as

$$\begin{aligned} & (2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{b}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \times(3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ & =(2 \bar{a}+3 \bar{b}+\bar{c}) \cdot[3(\bar{a} \times \bar{a})+(\bar{a} \times \bar{b})+2(\bar{a} \times \bar{c}) \\ & +6(\bar{b} \times \bar{a})+2(\bar{b} \times \bar{b})+4(\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) \\ & +9(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+3(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})+6(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ & =(2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})[0+(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})+2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \\ & -6(\bar{a} \times \bar{b})+0+4(\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) \\ & -9(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})-3(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+0] \\ & =(2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})[-5(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})+(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})-7(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ & =-10[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})]+2[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})]-14[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ & -15[\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})]+3[\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})]-21[\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ & -5[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})]+[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})]-7[\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ & =0+2[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{c}]+0 \\ & +0+0+21[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}] \\ & -5[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]+0+0 \\ & =18[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}] \\ \therefore \quad & 18[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=54 \\ & \Rightarrow[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \overline{\mathrm{b}} \overline{\mathrm{c}}]=3 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The volume of parallelopiped, whose coterminous edges are given by $$\overline{\mathrm{u}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{v}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \bar{w}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ is 1 cu. units. If $$\theta$$ is the angle between $$\bar{u}$$ and $$\bar{w}$$, then the value of $$\cos \theta$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Volume of parallelepiped }=\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{w}} \end{array}\right] \\ & \quad\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & \lambda \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=2 \\ & \therefore \quad \cos \theta=\frac{2+1+2}{\sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{6}}=\frac{5}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}=\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{k}}, \bar{b}=x \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(1-x) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$$ and $$\bar{c}=y \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+x \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(1+x-y) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$$, then $$[\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}]$$ depends on
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \bar{a}=\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \equiv(1,0,-1) \\\\ & \bar{b}=x \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(1-x) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \equiv(x, 1,1-x) \\\\ & \bar{c}=y \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+x \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(1+x-y) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \equiv(y, x, 1+x-y) \\\\ & \therefore\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \bar{a} & \bar{b} & \bar{c} \end{array}\right]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ x & 1 & 1-x \\ y & x & 1+x-y \end{array}\right| \\\\ & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ x & 1 & 1 \\ y & x & 1+x \end{array}\right| \quad \ldots \text { by } C_1+C_3 \\\\ & =1[(1)(1+x)-(x)(1)] \\\\ & =1+x-x \\\\ & =1 \\\\ & =\text { contains neither } x \text { nor } y \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\bar{a}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\bar{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ be two vectors. If $$\bar{c}$$ is a vector such that $$\bar{b} \times \bar{c}=\bar{b} \times \bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{c} \cdot \bar{a}=0$$, then $$\bar{c} \cdot \bar{b}$$ is equal to

The magnitude of the projection of the vector $$2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+ 3\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ on the vector perpendicular to the plane containing the vectors $$\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ is
explanation:
To find the vector perpendicular to the plane containing the vectors $$\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2\hat{\mathbf{j}}+3\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$, we can use the cross product of these two vectors.

The cross product is defined as:

$$\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = (a_2b_3 - a_3b_2)\hat{\mathbf{i}} + (a_3b_1 - a_1b_3)\hat{\mathbf{j}} + (a_1b_2 - a_2b_1)\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

For our vectors, this becomes:

$$\begin{aligned} (\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times (\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2\hat{\mathbf{j}}+3\hat{\mathbf{k}}) &= \left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ \end{array}\right| \\ &= \hat{\mathbf{i}}(1 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 2) - \hat{\mathbf{j}}(1 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 1) + \hat{\mathbf{k}}(1 \cdot 2 - 1 \cdot 1) \\\\ &= \hat{\mathbf{i}}(3 - 2) - \hat{\mathbf{j}}(3 - 1) + \hat{\mathbf{k}}(2 - 1) \\\\ &= \hat{\mathbf{i}}(1) - \hat{\mathbf{j}}(2) + \hat{\mathbf{k}}(1) \\\\ &= \hat{\mathbf{i}} - 2\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{k}}. \end{aligned}$$

Now that we have the perpendicular vector, let us compute the magnitude of the projection of the vector $$2\hat{\mathbf{i}}+ 3\hat{\mathbf{j}}+ \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ onto $$\hat{\mathbf{i}} - 2\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$.

The projection of vector $$\mathbf{a}$$ onto vector $$\mathbf{b}$$ is given by the formula:

$$ \text{proj}_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{a}) = \frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\|\mathbf{b}\|} $$

But we are only interested in the magnitude of the projection, which simplifies to:

$$ \|\text{proj}_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{a})\| = \left| \frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\|\mathbf{b}\|} \right| $$

Let's find the dot product of vectors $$2\hat{\mathbf{i}}+ 3\hat{\mathbf{j}}+ \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\hat{\mathbf{i}} - 2\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$:

$$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} &= (2\hat{\mathbf{i}}+ 3\hat{\mathbf{j}}+ \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot (\hat{\mathbf{i}} - 2\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\\\ &= 2(1) - 3(2) + 1(1) \\\\ &= 2 - 6 + 1 \\\\ &= -3. \end{aligned}$$

Next, let's find the magnitude of $$\mathbf{b}$$, which is $$\|\mathbf{b}\|$$:

$$\begin{aligned} \|\mathbf{b}\| &= \sqrt{(\hat{\mathbf{i}} - 2\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot (\hat{\mathbf{i}} - 2\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{k}})} \\\\ &= \sqrt{1^2 + (-2)^2 + 1^2} \\\\ &= \sqrt{1 + 4 + 1} \\\\ &= \sqrt{6}. \end{aligned}$$

Finally, the magnitude of the projection is:

$$\begin{aligned} \|\text{proj}_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{a})\| &= \left| \frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\|\mathbf{b}\|} \right| \\\\ &= \left| \frac{-3}{\sqrt{6}} \right| \\\\ &= \left| -\frac{\sqrt{9}}{\sqrt{6}} \right| \\\\ &= \left| -\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \right| \\\\ &= \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}=\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{k}}, \bar{b}=\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$$ and $$\bar{c}=\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$$ are three vectors then vector $$\bar{r}$$ in the plane of $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$, whose projection on $$\bar{c}$$ is $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$, is given by
explanation:
Let $$\bar{r} = x\bar{a} + y\bar{b}$$. Since $$\bar{r}$$ lies in the plane of $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$, it can be expressed as a linear combination of $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$.

The projection of $$\bar{r}$$ on $$\bar{c}$$ is given by:

$$ \frac{\bar{r} \cdot \bar{c}}{||\bar{c}||} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Substituting the values of $$\bar{r}$$ and $$\bar{c}$$ we get:

$$ \frac{(x\bar{a} + y\bar{b}) \cdot (\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{k}})}{||\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{k}}||} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

$$ \frac{(x(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{k}}) + y(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{k}})) \cdot (\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{k}})}{\sqrt{1^2 + (-1)^2 + (-1)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Simplifying the dot product and solving for $$x + y$$ we get:

$$x + y = 1$$

Now we can express $$\bar{r}$$ as:

$$ \bar{r} = x(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{k}}) + (1-x)(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+\hat{\boldsymbol{k}}) $$

$$ \bar{r} = (2x-1)\hat{\boldsymbol{i}} + (2-2x)\hat{\boldsymbol{j}} + \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$$

Checking the options, we find that option C, $$3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$ satisfies the condition $$x + y = 1$$ and also the equation for $$\bar{r}$$. Therefore, the vector $$\bar{r}$$ is given by $$3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$.

content: The polar co-ordinates of the point, whose Cartesian coordinates are $$(-2 \sqrt{3}, 2)$$, are
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Cartesian } \equiv(-2 \sqrt{3}, 2) \equiv(r \cdot \cos \theta, r \cdot \sin \theta) \\ & \begin{aligned} & \therefore r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=\sqrt{12+4}=4 \\ & \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(-\tan \frac{\pi}{6}\right) \\ &= \tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right] \\ &= \tan ^{-1}\left(\tan \frac{5 \pi}{6}\right) \\ &= \frac{5 \pi}{6} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \text { Polar } \equiv\left(4, \frac{5 \pi}{6}\right)$$

content: For any non-zero vectors $$\bar{a}, \bar{b}, \bar{c}$$, the value of $$\bar{a} \cdot[(\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) \times(\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c})]$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \times(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ & =\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})+(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})+(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ & =\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(0)+\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})+\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(0) \\ & =0+\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+0-\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \bar{b}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+23 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{c}=7 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+23 \hat{k}$$, then which of the following is valid.
explanation:
We have $$\bar{a}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \bar{b}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+23 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{c}=7 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+23 \hat{k}$$

$$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}=6-1-23 \neq 0$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=14+1+529 \neq 0$$

Thus $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ are not mutually perpendicular.

Also for $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}, \frac{3}{2} \neq-1 \neq \frac{-1}{23}$$.

Thus $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ are not collinear.

Now $$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}3 & 1 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & 23 \\ 7 & -1 & 23\end{array}\right|=3(-23+23)-(46-161)-(-2+7) \neq 0$$.

Thus $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ are non coplanar.

content: If the angle between the vectors $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=2 \lambda^2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ is obtuse, then $$\lambda \in$$
explanation:
We have $$\bar{a}=2 \lambda^2 \hat{i}+4 \lambda \hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{b}=7 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\lambda \hat{k}$$

$$\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}=|\bar{a}| \cdot|\bar{b}| \cdot \cos \theta$$, where $$\theta$$ is angle between $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$.

$$\therefore \cos \theta=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|}<0$$, as $\theta$ is an obtuse angle.

$$\therefore|\overline{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|<0$$

$$\therefore\left(2 \lambda^2\right)(7)+(4 \lambda)(-2)+(1)(\lambda)<0$$

$$\therefore 14 \lambda^2-7 \lambda<0 \Rightarrow 7 \lambda(2 \lambda-1)<0 \Rightarrow \lambda(2 \lambda-1)<0$$

$$\therefore 0<\lambda<\frac{1}{2}$$ i.e. $$\lambda \in\left(0, \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

content: If $$\bar{a}+\bar{b}, \bar{b}+\bar{c}, \bar{c}+\bar{a}$$ are coterminus edges of a parallelopiped, then its volume is
explanation:
To find the volume of the parallelepiped formed by the coterminus edges $$\bar{a}+\bar{b}, \bar{b}+\bar{c}, \bar{c}+\bar{a}$$, we need to evaluate the scalar triple product of these vectors. The scalar triple product for vectors $$\bar{u}, \bar{v}, \bar{w}$$ is given by the determinant:

$$ |\bar{u} \cdot (\bar{v} \times \bar{w})| $$

For the given vectors $$\bar{a}+\bar{b}, \bar{b}+\bar{c}, \bar{c}+\bar{a}$$, let's denote them as follows:

$$ \bar{u} = \bar{a}+\bar{b} $$

$$ \bar{v} = \bar{b}+\bar{c} $$

$$ \bar{w} = \bar{c}+\bar{a} $$

Now, we need to compute the scalar triple product $$ (\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \cdot ((\bar{b}+\bar{c}) \times (\bar{c}+\bar{a})) $$.

We start by finding the cross product $$ (\bar{b}+\bar{c}) \times (\bar{c}+\bar{a}) $$:

$$ (\bar{b}+\bar{c}) \times (\bar{c}+\bar{a}) = (\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) + (\bar{b} \times \bar{a}) + (\bar{c} \times \bar{c}) + (\bar{c} \times \bar{a}) $$

Since the cross product of any vector with itself is zero, $$ (\bar{c} \times \bar{c}) = 0 $$, we simplify this to:

$$ (\bar{b}+\bar{c}) \times (\bar{c}+\bar{a}) = (\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) + (\bar{b} \times \bar{a}) + (\bar{c} \times \bar{a}) $$

Next, we take the dot product of $$ \bar{a}+\bar{b} $$ with this result:

$$ (\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \cdot \left[ (\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) + (\bar{b} \times \bar{a}) + (\bar{c} \times \bar{a}) \right] $$

Using the distributive property of the dot product over addition, we have:

$$ (\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \cdot (\bar{b} \times \bar{c}) + (\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \cdot (\bar{b} \times \bar{a}) + (\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \cdot (\bar{c} \times \bar{a}) $$

We know that the dot product of a vector with the cross product of two other vectors follows the property that:

$$ \bar{x} \cdot (\bar{y} \times \bar{z}) = [\bar{x} \bar{y} \bar{z}] $$

Applying this property, we get:

$$ [(\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \bar{b} \bar{c}] + [(\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \bar{b} \bar{a}] + [(\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \bar{c} \bar{a}] $$

We evaluate each of these scalar triple products separately:

$$ [(\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \bar{b} \bar{c}] = [\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}] + [\bar{b} \bar{b} \bar{c}] = [\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}] + 0 = [\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}] $$

$$ [(\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \bar{b} \bar{a}] = [\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{a}] + [\bar{b} \bar{b} \bar{a}] = 0 + 0 = 0 $$

$$ [(\bar{a}+\bar{b}) \bar{c} \bar{a}] = [\bar{a} \bar{c} \bar{a}] + [\bar{b} \bar{c} \bar{a}] = 0 + [\bar{b} \bar{c} \bar{a}] = [\bar{b} \bar{c} \bar{a}] $$

Summing these, we have:

$$ [\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}] + 0 + [\bar{b} \bar{c} \bar{a}] = [\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}] + [\bar{b} \bar{c} \bar{a}] = 2[\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}] $$

Therefore, the volume of the parallelepiped is:

$$ 2[\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}] $$

Hence, the correct answer is Option D:

Option D: $$ 2[\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}] $$

content: $$\vec{a}=4 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}-18 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$ and $$\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}$$ are three vectors such that $$\vec{a}=x \vec{b}+y \vec{c}$$, then $$x+y=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \bar{a}=x \bar{b}+y \bar{c} \\ & \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 4 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}-18 \hat{k}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})(x)+(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})(y) \\ &=(x+2 y) \hat{i}+(-2 x+3 y) \hat{j}+(3 x-4 y) \hat{k} \\ & \therefore \quad x+2 y=4, \quad-2 x+3 y=13 \quad \text { and } \quad 3 x-4 y=-18 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Solving, we get $$x=-2, y=3 \Rightarrow x+y=1$$

content: If $$\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{c}=\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \vec{a} \times \bar{b}=\bar{c}$$ and $$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1$$, then $$\vec{b}$$
explanation:
Let $$\bar{b}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}$$ and we have $$\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})(x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k})=1 \Rightarrow x+y+z=1 \\ & \quad \bar{a} \times \bar{b}=\bar{c} \quad \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ x & y & z \end{array}\right|=\hat{j}-\hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore(z-y) \hat{i}-(z-x) \hat{j}+(y-x) \hat{k} \quad=\hat{j}-\hat{k} \\ & \therefore z-y=0, x-z=1, y-x=-1 \\ & \therefore y=z, z=x-1, y=x-1 \\ & \text { Thus } \bar{b}=x \hat{i}+(x-1) \hat{j}+(x-1) \hat{k} \\ & \text { We have } x+y+z=1 \\ & \therefore x+(x-1)+(x-1)=1 \Rightarrow x=1, y=0, z=0 \\ & \therefore \bar{b}=\hat{i} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the vectors $$\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+p \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$$ and $$\vec{b}=6 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+q \hat{k}$$ are collinear, then $$p$$ and $$q$$ are
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \bar{a}=x \bar{b} \\ & \therefore 2 \hat{i}+p \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}=6 x \hat{i}-9 x \hat{j}+q x \hat{j} \\ & \therefore 2=6 x \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{3} \\ & \quad p=-9 x \Rightarrow p=(-9)\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)=-3 \text { and } 4=q x=q\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \Rightarrow q=4(3)=12 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$|\vec{a}|=4,|\vec{b}|=5$$, then the values of $$k$$ for which $$\vec{a}+k \vec{b}$$ is perpendicular to $$\vec{a}-k \vec{b}$$ are
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & (\bar{a}+k \bar{b}) \cdot(\bar{a}-k \bar{b})=0 \\ & \therefore|a|^2-k^2|\bar{b}|^2=0 \\ & \therefore(4)^2-k^2(5)^2=0 \Rightarrow k= \pm \frac{4}{5} \end{aligned}$$

content: The vectors $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ and $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ are the sides of a triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$. The length of the median through $$\mathrm{A}$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{A}$$ be the origin.

Then $$\mathrm{B}=(3,0,4)$$ and $$\mathrm{C}=(5,-2,4)$$

Mid point of $$\mathrm{BC}=\left(\frac{3+5}{2}, \frac{0-2}{2}, \frac{4+4}{2}\right)$$ i.e. $$(4,-1,4)$$

$$\therefore$$ Length of median $$=\sqrt{(4)^2+(-1)^2+(4)^2}=\sqrt{33}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}, \bar{b}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{c}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$$, then $$(\bar{a} \times \bar{b}) \cdot(\bar{a} \times \bar{c})=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \bar{a} \times \bar{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -4 \end{array}\right|=\hat{i}(-10)-\hat{j}(-9)+\hat{k}(7)=-10 \hat{i}+9 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k} \\ & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & 3 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right|=\hat{\mathrm{i}}(-5)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(-3)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(-1)=-5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & (\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \quad=(-10 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+9 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(-5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=50+27-7=70 \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}$$. If $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ is a vector such that $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|,|\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=2 \sqrt{2}$$ and the angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ is $$60^{\circ}$$. Then $$|(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}|=$$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \begin{array}{l} \overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}} \mid=\sqrt{4+4+1}=3 . \text { Also }|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=\sqrt{4+1+4}=3 \\ \overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}}=2 \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow(\overline{\mathrm{c}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}})^2=8 \\ \therefore|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2-2 \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=8 \\ \therefore|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+9-2|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=8 \quad \cdots[\because \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=|\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \text {, given }] \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2-2|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|+1 \quad=0 \quad \Rightarrow(|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|-1)^2=0 \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=1 \\ & |(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}| \quad=|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot|\overline{\mathrm{c}}| \cdot \sin 60^{\circ}=(3)(1)\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The projection of $$\bar{a}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ on $$\bar{b}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ is
explanation: Projection $$\bar{a}$$ on $$\bar{b}=\frac{\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}}{|\bar{b}|}=\frac{(\mathrm{i}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{(2)^2+(-1)^2+(1)^2}}=\frac{2+2+1}{\sqrt{6}}=\frac{5}{\sqrt{6}}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \bar{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{c}=3 \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}$$ are coplanar, then $$\lambda$$ is the root of the equation
explanation:
Since given vectors are coplanar, we write

$$\begin{aligned} &\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & \lambda & 5 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore 2(10+3 \lambda)+1(5+9)+1(\lambda-6)=0 \\ & \therefore 20+6 \lambda+14+\lambda-6=0 \Rightarrow 7 \lambda+28=0 \quad \Rightarrow \lambda=-4 \end{aligned}$$

Put $$x=-4$$ in all options.

(A) $$8-12=-4 \neq 6$$

(B) $$16-8=8 \neq 4$$

(C) $$16-12=4$$

(D) $$16-8=8 \neq 6$$

content: If $$\hat{a}$$ is a unit vector such that $$(\bar{x}-\hat{a}) \cdot(\bar{x}+\hat{a})=8$$, then $$|\bar{x}|=$$
explanation:
We have $$(\bar{x}-\hat{a}) \cdot(\bar{x}+\hat{a})=8$$

$$\therefore|\bar{x}|^2-|\hat{a}|^2=8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad|\bar{x}|^2=8+1=9 \quad \Rightarrow|\bar{x}|=3$$

content: Let $$\vec{v}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{w}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{k}$$. If $$\bar{u}$$ is a unit vector, then the maximum value of the scalar triple product $$[\bar{u} \bar{v} \bar{w}]$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \bar{v} \times \bar{w}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right|=\hat{i}(6)-\hat{j}(7)+\hat{k}(-2)=6 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} \\ & \therefore|\bar{v} \times \bar{w}|=\sqrt{(6)^2+(7)^2+(2)^2}=\sqrt{89} \\ & \text { Also }|\bar{u}|=1 \\ & \therefore\left[\begin{array}{lll} \bar{u} & \bar{v} & \bar{w} \end{array}\right]=\sqrt{89} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ is perpendicular to $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$, then $$\lambda=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & (\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=0 \\ & \therefore[(1-\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(2+2 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(3+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}}] \cdot[3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}] \quad=0 \\ & \therefore(1-\lambda)(3)+(2+2 \lambda)(1)=0 \quad \Rightarrow \lambda=5 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$3 \hat{j}, 4 \hat{k}$$ and $$3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$$ are the position vectors of the vertices $$A, B, C$$ respectively of $$\triangle A B C$$, then the position vector of the point in which the bisector of $$\angle \mathrm{A}$$ meets $$\mathrm{BC}$$ is
explanation:
We have $$\bar{a}=3 \hat{j}, \bar{b}=4 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{c}=3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$$

Angle bisector $$\mathrm{AD}$$ divides $$\mathrm{BC}$$ in the ratio $$\mathrm{AB}$$ : $$\mathrm{AC}$$.

$$\mathrm{A B}=\sqrt{9+16}=5 \quad$$ and $$\quad \mathrm{A C}=\sqrt{0+16}=4$$

Thus D divides $$\mathrm{BC}$$ in the ratio $$5: 4$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \mathrm{D} & =\left[0, \frac{5(3)}{5+4}, \frac{5(4)+4(4)}{5+4}\right] \\ & =\left(0, \frac{15}{9}, \frac{20+16}{9}\right)=\left(0, \frac{5}{3}, 4\right) \Rightarrow \frac{5}{3} \hat{j}+4 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the vectors $$2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k} ; \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$ and $$3 \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}$$ are coplanar, then the value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
For three vectors to be coplanar, the scalar triple product of the vectors must equal zero. The scalar triple product of vectors $$\vec{a}, \vec{b},$$ and $$\vec{c}$$ can be found by calculating the determinant of a matrix composed of their components. If the vectors are $$\vec{a} = 2\hat{i} - \hat{j} - \hat{k}$$, $$\vec{b} = \hat{i} + 2\hat{j} - 3\hat{k}$$, and $$\vec{c} = 3\hat{i} + \lambda\hat{j} + 5\hat{k}$$, we can set up the following determinant:

$$ \begin{vmatrix} 2 & -1 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & \lambda & 5 \\ \end{vmatrix} $$

To solve the determinant, we expand it as follows:


$$ = 2(2\cdot5 - (-3)\cdot\lambda) - (-1)(1\cdot5 - (-3)\cdot3) + (-1)(1\cdot\lambda - 2\cdot3) $$

$$ = 2(10 + 3\lambda) - (-1)(5 + 9) - (1\cdot\lambda - 6) $$

$$ = 20 + 6\lambda + 14 - \lambda + 6 $$

$$ = 40 + 5\lambda $$

For the vectors to be coplanar, this determinant must equal zero, so we set the expression equal to zero:

$$ 40 + 5\lambda = 0 $$

Solving for $$\lambda$$:

$$ 5\lambda = -40 $$

$$ \lambda = -\frac{40}{5} $$

$$ \lambda = -8 $$

Therefore, $$\lambda = -8$$, which corresponds to Option A.

content: The vector equation of the line whose Cartesian equations are $$y=2$$ and $$4 x-3 z+5=0$$ is
explanation:
We have $$4 x-3 z+5=0$$ and $$y=2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 4 x=3 z-5 \Rightarrow 4 x=3\left(z-\frac{5}{3}\right) \\ & \therefore \frac{4 x}{12}=\frac{3\left(z-\frac{5}{3}\right)}{12} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}=\frac{\left(z-\frac{5}{3}\right)}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Thus line passes through point $$\left(0,2, \frac{5}{3}\right)$$, and has direction ratios $$3,0,4$$.

Hence required equation of line is $$\left(2 \hat{j}+\frac{5}{3} \hat{k}\right)+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{k})$$.

content: The position vector of the point of inersection of the medians of a triangle, whose vertices are $$A(1,2,3), B(1,0,3)$$ and $$C(4,1,-3)$$ is
explanation:
To find the position vector of the centroid of a triangle, whose vertices are given as $ A(1,2,3) $, $ B(1,0,3) $, and $ C(4,1,-3) $, we use the formula for the centroid of a triangle. The centroid $ G $ has the coordinates, which are the arithmetic mean of the coordinates of the vertices of the triangle.

Mathematically, the coordinates of the centroid $ G(x, y, z) $ are given by:

$$ x = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + x_3}{3} $$

$$ y = \frac{y_1 + y_2 + y_3}{3} $$

$$ z = \frac{z_1 + z_2 + z_3}{3} $$

Substituting the given vertices $ A(1,2,3) $, $ B(1,0,3) $, and $ C(4,1,-3) $:

$$ x = \frac{1 + 1 + 4}{3} = \frac{6}{3} = 2 $$

$$ y = \frac{2 + 0 + 1}{3} = \frac{3}{3} = 1 $$

$$ z = \frac{3 + 3 + (-3)}{3} = \frac{3}{3} = 1 $$

Therefore, the position vector of the centroid is:

$$ \vec{G} = 2 \hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k} $$

Thus, the correct choice is:

Option B

$$ 2 \hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k} $$

content: The area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are represented by the vectors $$\bar{a}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{b}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$$ is
explanation:
To find the area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are represented by the vectors $$\bar{a} = 3 \hat{i} - \hat{j} - 2 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{b} = -\hat{i} + 3 \hat{j} - 3 \hat{k}$$, we use the vector cross product. The area of a parallelogram formed by two vectors is twice the area of the triangle formed by those vectors, and it can be calculated using the cross product of the vectors representing the diagonals.

The formula for the area of a parallelogram with diagonals represented by vectors $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$ is given by:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left\| \bar{a} \times \bar{b} \right\| $$

First, we need to find the cross product $$\bar{a} \times \bar{b}$$:

$$ \bar{a} \times \bar{b} = \left| \begin{matrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -1 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & -3 \\ \end{matrix} \right| $$

Expanding this determinant, we get:

$$ \bar{a} \times \bar{b} = \hat{i} \left( (-1)(-3) - (-2)(3) \right) - \hat{j} \left( 3(-3) - (-2)(-1) \right) + \hat{k} \left( 3(3) - (-1)(-1) \right) $$

$$ = \hat{i} (3 + 6) - \hat{j} (-9 - 2) + \hat{k} (9 - 1) $$

$$ = \hat{i} (9) - \hat{j} (-11) + \hat{k} (8) $$

$$ = 9 \hat{i} + 11 \hat{j} + 8 \hat{k} $$

So, the cross product $$\bar{a} \times \bar{b}$$ is $$9 \hat{i} + 11 \hat{j} + 8 \hat{k}$$.

Next, to find the magnitude of this vector, we use the formula:

$$ \left\| \bar{a} \times \bar{b} \right\| = \sqrt{(9)^2 + (11)^2 + (8)^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{81 + 121 + 64} $$

$$ = \sqrt{266} $$

Therefore, the area of the parallelogram is:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{266} = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{266} \text{ sq. units} $$

The correct option is Option B $$\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{266}$$ sq. units.

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\overline{0}$$ with $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=3,|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=5$$ and $$|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=7$$, then angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ is
explanation:
To find the angle between the vectors $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$, we start with the given equation:

$$\overline{\mathrm{a}} + \overline{\mathrm{b}} + \overline{\mathrm{c}} = \overline{0}.$$

This implies that $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} + \overline{\mathrm{b}} = -\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$. Taking the magnitudes on both sides, we get:

$$|\overline{\mathrm{a}} + \overline{\mathrm{b}}| = |\overline{\mathrm{c}}|.$$

Since magnitudes are always non-negative, and given the magnitudes of the vectors $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}| = 3$$, $$|\overline{\mathrm{b}}| = 5$$, and $$|\overline{\mathrm{c}}| = 7$$, we can apply the law of cosines:

$$|\overline{\mathrm{a}} + \overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2 = |\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2 + |\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2 + 2 |\overline{\mathrm{a}}| |\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cos{\theta},$$

where $$\theta$$ is the angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$. Plugging in the given magnitudes:

$$7^2 = 3^2 + 5^2 + 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot \cos{\theta}.$$

Simplify this equation:

$$49 = 9 + 25 + 30 \cos{\theta}.$$

Combine like terms:

$$49 = 34 + 30 \cos{\theta}.$$

Isolate the cosine term:

$$49 - 34 = 30 \cos{\theta},$$

$$15 = 30 \cos{\theta},$$

Therefore:

$$\cos{\theta} = \frac{1}{2}.$$

The angle whose cosine is $$\frac{1}{2}$$ is:

$$\theta = \frac{\pi}{3}.$$

Thus, the angle between the vectors $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ is:

Option A: $$\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{\mathrm{c}}$$
