content: If $$|\bar{u}|=2$$ and $$\bar{u}$$ makes angles of $$60^{\circ}$$ and $$120^{\circ}$$ with axes $$\mathrm{OX}$$ and $$\mathrm{OY}$$ in the origin, then $$\bar{u}=$$
explanation:
To find the vector $$\bar{u}$$ given the magnitude and angles with the axes, let's break it down step by step.

We know the following:

  • Magnitude of $$\bar{u}$$: $$|\bar{u}| = 2$$
  • Angle with the x-axis (OX): $$60^{\circ}$$
  • Angle with the y-axis (OY): $$120^{\circ}$$

We can represent $$\bar{u}$$ in component form as:

$$ \bar{u} = u_x \hat{i} + u_y \hat{j} + u_z \hat{k} $$

To find the components $$u_x$$ and $$u_y$$, we use the angles with the axes:

For the x-component (using $$60^{\circ}$$):

$$ u_x = |\bar{u}| \cos(60^{\circ}) = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 1 $$

For the y-component (using $$120^{\circ}$$):

$$ u_y = |\bar{u}| \cos(120^{\circ}) = 2 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) = -1 $$

We don't have information about the z-component directly, but we know that the total magnitude should be 2. Thus, we use the Pythagorean theorem in 3-dimensional space:

$$ |\bar{u}| = \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2 + u_z^2} $$

$$ 2 = \sqrt{1^2 + (-1)^2 + u_z^2} $$

$$ 2 = \sqrt{1 + 1 + u_z^2} $$

$$ 2 = \sqrt{2 + u_z^2} $$

Squaring both sides:

$$ 4 = 2 + u_z^2 $$

$$ u_z^2 = 2 $$

$$ u_z = \pm \sqrt{2} $$

Therefore, the vector $$\bar{u}$$ is:

$$ \bar{u} = 1\hat{i} - 1\hat{j} \pm \sqrt{2}\hat{k} $$

So, the correct option is:

Option D: $$2(\hat{i} - \hat{j} \pm \sqrt{2} \hat{k})$$

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ are mutually perpendicular vectors having magnitudes $$1,2,3$$ respectively, then $$\left[\begin{array}{lll}\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} & \overline{\mathrm{b}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}}\end{array}\right]=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & {[\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \quad \overline{\mathrm{b}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}} \quad \overline{\mathrm{c}}] } \\ = & (\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot\left[(\overline{\mathrm{b}}-\overline{\mathrm{a}})_{\times} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}}\right] \\ = & (\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})-(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})] \\ = & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})-\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+\overline{\mathrm{b}}(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \\ = & 2 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=2(1)(2)(3)=12\end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ is perpendicular to $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$, then $$\lambda=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \bar{a}+\lambda \bar{b} & =(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \\ & =(1+3 \lambda) \hat{i}+(2-\lambda) \hat{j}+(-3+2 \lambda) \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

Since $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ is $$\perp$$ er to $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & (1)(1+3 \lambda)+(3)(2-\lambda)+(1)(-3+2 \lambda)=0 \\ & \therefore 1+3 \lambda+6-3 \lambda-3+2 \lambda=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda=-2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $${\pi \over 2} < \theta < \pi $$ and $$|\overline a | = 5,|\overline b | = 13,|\overline a \times \overline b | = 25$$, then the value of $$\overline a \,.\,\overline b $$ is
explanation: $$\begin{array}{left}|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|=25 & \\ \therefore|\overline{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot \sin \theta=(5)(13) \sin \theta =25 & \\ \therefore \sin \theta =\frac{5}{13} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{-12}{13} & \ldots\left[\because \frac{\pi}{2}<\theta \leq \pi\right] \\ \therefore \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}} =|\overline{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot \cos \theta & \\ =(5)(13)\left(\frac{-12}{13}\right)=-60 & \end{array}$$

content: If $$|\bar{a} \times \bar{b}|^2+(\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b})^2=144$$ and $$|\bar{a}|=4$$, then $$|\bar{b}|=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & |\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}})^2=144 \text { and }|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=4 \\ & \therefore\left(|\mathrm{a}|^2 \cdot|\mathrm{b}|^2 \cdot \sin ^2 \theta\right)+\left(|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2 \cos ^2 \theta\right)=144 \\ & \therefore|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta\right)=144 \\ & \therefore(4)^2|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2=144 \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2=9 \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=3\end{aligned}$$

content:
The distance between parallel lines

$$\begin{aligned} & \bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \text { and } \\ & \bar{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \text { is } \end{aligned}$$
explanation:
The distance between given parallel lines

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left|\frac{[(\hat{i}-2 \hat{i})+(-\hat{j}+\hat{j})+(2 \hat{k}-\hat{k})] \times(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k})}{|2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}|}\right|=\left|\frac{(-\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{(2)^2+(1)^2+(-2)^2}}\right| \\ & (-\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k})=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right|=\hat{i}(0-1)-\hat{j}(2-2)+\hat{k}(-1-0)=-\hat{i}-\hat{k} \\ & \therefore d=\left|\frac{|-\hat{i}-\hat{k}|}{\sqrt{9}}\right|=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{3} \text { units } \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The vertices of triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$ are $$\mathrm{A} \equiv(3,0,0) ; \mathrm{B} \equiv(0,0,4) ; \mathrm{C} \equiv(0,5,4)$$. Find the position vector of the point in which the bisector of angle A meets B C is
explanation:
Refer figure

Let AD be the angle bisector of angle A which divides BC in the ratio AB : AC.

Here AB = $$\sqrt{9+16}=\sqrt{25}$$ and

AC = $$\sqrt{9+25+16}$$

$$=\sqrt{50}$$

$$\therefore$$ D divides BC in the ratio $$\sqrt{25} : \sqrt{50}$$ i.e. 1 : 2

$$\therefore$$ Position vector of $$D=\frac{(4)(2) \hat{k}+5 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{1+2}=\frac{5 j+12 \hat{k}}{3}$$

content: In a quadrilateral PQRS, M and N are mid-points of the sides PQ and RS respectively. If $$\overline {PS} + \overline {QR} = t\overline {MN} $$, then t =
explanation:
Refer figure.

$$\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r}, \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n}$$ be the position vectors of points $$\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}, \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{M}, \mathrm{N}$$.

Now $$\overline{\mathrm{PS}}+\overline{\mathrm{QR}}$$

$$=\overline{\mathrm{s}}-\overline{\mathrm{p}}+\overline{\mathrm{r}}-\overline{\mathrm{q}}=(\overline{\mathrm{s}}+\overline{\mathrm{r}})-(\overline{\mathrm{p}}+\overline{\mathrm{q}})$$

Since $$M$$ and $$N$$ are mid-points of $$P Q$$ and RS respectively, we write

$$\overline{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{p}}+\overline{\mathrm{q}}}{2} \text { and } \overline{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{r}}+\overline{\mathrm{s}}}{2}$$

$$\therefore$$ eq. (1) becomes

$$\overline{\mathrm{PS}}+\overline{\mathrm{QR}}=2 \overline{\mathrm{n}}-2 \overline{\mathrm{m}}=2(\overline{\mathrm{n}}-\overline{\mathrm{m}})=2 \overline{\mathrm{MN}}$$

From given data, $$t=2$$

content: If vectors $$\bar{a}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \bar{b}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{c}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$$ are such that, $$\bar{a}+\lambda \bar{b}$$ is perpendicular to $$\bar{c}$$, then $$\lambda=$$
explanation:
From given data, we write

$$\bar{a}+\lambda \bar{b}=(2-\lambda) \hat{i}+(2+2 \lambda) \hat{j}+(3+\lambda) \hat{k}$$ ..... (1)

Since (1) is $$\perp$$ er to $$\bar{c}$$, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & (2-\lambda)(3)+(2+2 \lambda)(1)+(3+\lambda)(2)=0 \\ & \therefore 6-3 \lambda+2+2 \lambda+6+2 \lambda=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-14 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\bar{a}=3 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}, \bar{b}=6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$$ are two vectors and $$\bar{c}$$ is a vector such that $$\bar{c}=\bar{a} \times \bar{b}$$, then $$a: b$$ : is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \bar{c}=\bar{a} \times \bar{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -5 & 0 \\ 6 & 3 & 0\end{array}\right|=\hat{i}(0)-\hat{j}(0)+\hat{k}(9+30)=39 \hat{k} \\ & |\bar{a}|=\sqrt{(3)^2+(-5)^2}=\sqrt{34} \text { and }|\bar{b}|=\sqrt{(6)^2+(3)^2}=\sqrt{45} \text { and }|\bar{c}|=\sqrt{39^2}=39 \\ & \therefore a: b: c=\sqrt{34}: \sqrt{45}: 39\end{aligned}$$

$$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2=|\overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+4 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ ..... (1)

Now $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2=|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\mathrm{b}|^2-2 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$

$$\therefore(5)^2=(3)^2+(4)^2-2 \bar{a} \cdot \bar{b} \Rightarrow \bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}=0$$

Substituting in (1), we get

$$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2 \quad=|\overline{\mathrm{a}}-\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2 \Rightarrow|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=5$$

content: $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ are vectors such that $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=5,|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=4,|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=3$$ and each is perpendicular to the sum of the other two, then $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=$$
explanation:
We have $$\bar{a} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})=0, \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}})=0$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}})=0$$

$$\therefore \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=0$$ ....... (1)

$$\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=0$$ ..... (2)

$$\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}=0$$ ..... (3)

From (1), (2) and (3), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad 2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}})=0 \\ & \text { Now }|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2+2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \\ & \therefore|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=(5)^2+(4)^2+(3)^2+2(0)=50 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$[\bar{a} \bar{b} \bar{c}]=4$$, then the volume (in cubic units) of the parallelopiped with $$\bar{a}+2 \bar{b}, \bar{b}+2 \bar{c}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ as coterminal edges, is
explanation:
We have $$\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=4$$

Volume of required parallelopiped is

$$\begin{aligned} & (\overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{b}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{c}}) \times(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{a}})] \\ & =(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot[(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+2(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+2(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+4(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})] \\ & =\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+\overline{\mathrm{a}}(0)+2 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+4 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+2 \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+4 \overline{\mathrm{b}}(0)+4 \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+8 \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \\ & =\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+0+0+0+0+0+0+8 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})=9[\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})]=9(4)=36 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ are three vectors such that $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}=\overline{0}$$ and $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|=3,|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|=5,|\overline{\mathrm{c}}|=7$$, then the angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$ is
explanation:
$$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}=0 \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{c}}=-(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}})$$ and let angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ be $$\theta$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore|\bar{c}|^2 & =(\bar{a}+\bar{b})^2=|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+2 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}} \\ & =|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+2|\overline{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\overline{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot \cos \theta \\ \therefore(7)^2 & =(3)^2+(5)^2+2(3)(5) \cos \theta \\ \therefore 49 & =9+25+30 \cos \theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{15}{30}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If area of the parallelogram with $$\bar{a}$$ and $$\bar{b}$$ as two adjacent sides is 20 square units, then the area of the parallelogram having $$3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ and $$2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ as two adjacent sides in square units is
explanation:
We have $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}|=20$$ and we have to find value of $$|(3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times(2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{b}})|$$

$$\begin{aligned} & (3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \times(2 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \\ & =6(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+9(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})+2(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+3(\overline{\mathrm{b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \\ & =0+9(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})-2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}})+0=7(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \times \overline{\mathrm{b}}) \end{aligned}$$

Hence area of the required parallelogram $$=7 \times 20=140$$

content: If $$\bar{r}=-4 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$$ is a linear combination of the vectors $$\bar{a}=-\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$ and $$\bar{b}=-8 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$$, then
explanation:
Let $$r=x \bar{a}+y \bar{b}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}=x(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+y(-8 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & =(-x-8 y) \hat{i}+(4 x-y) \hat{j}+(3 x+3 y) \hat{k} \\ & \therefore-x-8 y=-4 \quad ......\text{(1)}\\ & 4 x-y=-6 \quad ......\text{(2)}\\ & 3 \mathrm{x}+3 \mathrm{y}=-2\quad ......\text{(3)} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

Solving (1) and (2), we get

$$y=\frac{2}{3} \text { and } x=\frac{-4}{3}$$

content: If the volume of a tetrahedron whose conterminous edges are $$\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}, \vec{\mathrm{c}}+\vec{\mathrm{a}}$$ is 24 cubic units, then the volume of parallelopiped whose coterminous edges are $$\vec{\mathrm{a}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}, \vec{\mathrm{c}}$$ is
explanation:
As per data given, we write

$$24 = {1 \over 6}\left\{ {\left( {\overline a + \overline b } \right)\,.\,\left[ {\left( {\overline b + \overline c } \right) \times \left( {\overline c + \overline a } \right)} \right]} \right\}$$

$$ = {1 \over 6}\left\{ {\left( {\overline a + \overline b } \right)\,.\,\left[ {\left( {\overline b + \overline c } \right) + \left( {\overline b + \overline a } \right) + \left( {\overline c + \overline a } \right)} \right]} \right\}$$

... [$$\because$$ $$\overline c \times \overline c = 0$$]

$$\therefore$$ $$144 = \left[ {\overline a \,.\,\left( {\overline b \times \overline c } \right)} \right] + \left[ {\overline b \,.\,\left( {\overline b \times \overline c } \right)} \right] + \left[ {\overline a \,.\,\left( {\overline b \times \overline a } \right)} \right] + \left[ {\overline b \,.\,\left( {\overline b \times \overline a } \right)} \right] + \left[ {\overline a \,.\,\left( {\overline c \times \overline a } \right)} \right] + \left[ {\overline b \,.\,\left( {\overline c \times \overline a } \right)} \right]$$

$$ = \left[ {\overline a \,.\,\left( {\overline b \times \overline c } \right)} \right] + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + \left[ {\overline b \,.\,\left( {\overline c \times \overline a } \right)} \right]$$

$$ = 2\left[ {\overline a \,.\,\left( {\overline b \times \overline c } \right)} \right]$$ ...... [$$\because$$ $$\overline b \,.\,\,\left( {\overline c \times \overline a } \right) = \overline a \,.\,\,\left( {\overline b \times \overline c } \right)$$]

$$\therefore$$ $$\overline a \,.\,\,\left( {\overline b \times \overline c } \right) = 72$$, i.e. volume of parallelopiped with conterminous edges $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}$$.

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{e}}_1, \overline{\mathrm{e}}_2$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{e}}_1+\overline{\mathrm{e}}_2$$ are unit vectors, then the angle between $$\overline{\mathrm{e}}_1$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{e}}_2$$ is
explanation:
$$\left|\overline{\mathrm{e}}_1+\overline{\mathrm{e}}_2\right|^2=\left|\overline{\mathrm{e}}_1^2\right|+\left|\overline{\mathrm{e}}_2^2\right|+2 \overline{\mathrm{e}}_1 \overline{\mathrm{e}}_2 \cos \theta$$

Here $$\left|\overline{\mathrm{e}}_1\right|=1,\left|\overline{\mathrm{e}}_2\right|=1$$ and $$\left|\overline{\mathrm{e}}_1+\overline{\mathrm{e}}_2\right|=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(1)=(1)^2+(1)^2+2(1)(1) \cos \theta \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{-1}{2}=\cos \theta \quad \Rightarrow \theta=120^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}, \overline{\mathrm{b}} , \overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ are three vectors which are perpendicular to $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}, \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ and $$\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ respectively, such that $$|\bar{a}|=2,|\bar{b}|=3,|\bar{c}|=4$$, then $$|\bar{a}+\bar{b}+\bar{c}|=$$
explanation:
$$\overline{\mathrm{a}}$$ is $$\perp$$ er to $$\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}})=0 \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}=0$$ ..... (1)

$$\overline{\mathrm{b}}$$ is $$\perp$$ er to $$(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}}) \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}})=0 \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}=0$$ ..... (2)

$$\overline{\mathrm{c}}$$ is $$\perp$$ er to $$(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}})=0 \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}=0$$ ...... (3)

From (1), (2) and (3), we get

$$2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}})=0$$ ...... (4)

Now $$|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2=|\overline{\mathrm{a}}|^2+|\overline{\mathrm{b}}|^2+|\mathrm{c}|^2+2(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{a}})$$

$$\therefore|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}|^2 =(2)^2+(3)^2+(4)^2+0$$ ..... [From (4) and data given]

$$=4+9+16=29$$

$$\therefore|\overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}| =\sqrt{29}$$

content: $$(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+27 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \times(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mu \hat{\mathrm{k}})=\overline{0}$$, then $$\lambda$$ and $$\mu$$ are respectively
explanation:
From given data, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & 6 & 27 \\ 1 & \lambda & \mu \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \therefore \hat{\mathrm{i}}(6 \mu-27 \lambda)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(2 \mu-27)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(2 \lambda-6)=0 \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ 6$$\mu$$ $$-$$ 27$$\lambda$$ = 0 ..... (1)

27 $$-$$ 2$$\mu$$ = 0 ..... (2)

2$$\lambda$$ $$-$$ 6 = 0 ..... (3)

From (2) and (3), we get $$\mu=\frac{27}{2}$$ and $$\lambda=3$$.

These values of $$\lambda$$ & $$\mu$$ satisfy eq. (1)

content: If $\mathbf{a}=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \mathbf{b}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\mathbf{c}=7 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+23 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ are three vectors, then which of the following statement is true.

options.content: a, b, c are mutually perpendicular
a, b and c are coplanar
a, b and c are non-coplanar

explanation:
Given, vectors

$$\mathbf{a}=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \mathbf{b}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{and}\quad & \mathbf{c}=7 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+23 \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ & \mathbf{a} \neq \lambda \mathbf{b} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad \mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are not collinear

$\therefore \quad$ Option (a) is false

$$\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=6-1-7 \neq 0$$

$\therefore \quad$ a and $b$ are not perpendicular

$\therefore \quad \mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}$ are not mutually perpendicular

$\therefore$ Option (b) is also incorrect.

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \mathbf{a} \cdot(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & 1 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & 7 \\ 7 & -1 & 23 \end{array}\right| \\ & =3(-23+7)-1(46-49)-1(-2+7) \\ & =-48+3-5=-50 \neq 0 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad \mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{c}$ are non-coplanar

$\therefore \quad$ Option (d) IV is true.

content: $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are non-collinear vectors. If $p=(2 x+1) a-b$ and $q=(x-2) a+b$ are collinear vectors, then $x=$
explanation:
Given, a and b are non-collinear vector and $\mathbf{p}=(2 x+1) \mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{q}=(x-2) \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}$ are collinear vector.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \quad \frac{2 x+1}{x-2} =\frac{-1}{1} \\ \Rightarrow & \quad 2 x+1 =-x+2 \\ \Rightarrow & \quad3 x =1 \\ \Rightarrow & \quad x =\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}$ are non-coplanar vectors and $p=\frac{\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}}{[a b c]}, q=\frac{\mathbf{c} \times \mathbf{a}}{[a b c]}, r=\frac{\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}}{[a b c]}$, then $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{p}+\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{q}+\mathbf{c} \cdot \mathbf{r}=$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, } \mathrm{p}=\frac{\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}}{[\mathrm{abc}]}\\ &\begin{aligned} & q=\frac{c \times a}{[a b c]} \\ & \mathbf{r}=\frac{\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}}{[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}]} \\ & a \cdot p+b \cdot q+c \cdot r \\ & =\frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})}{[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}]}+\frac{\mathbf{b} \cdot(\mathbf{c} \times \mathbf{a})}{[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b}]}+\frac{\mathbf{c} \cdot(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b})}{[\mathbf{a b c}]} \\ & =1+1+1=3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$G$$ be the centroid of a $$\triangle A B C$$ and $$\mathrm{O}_{b_\theta}$$ other point in that plane, then $$\mathrm{OA}+\mathrm{OB}+\mathrm{OC}+\mathrm{CG}=$$
explanation:
Given,

$$G$$ be the centroid of a triangle $$A B C$$ and $$O$$ be any other point in that plane

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & O G=\frac{O A+O B+O C}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & O A+O B+O C=3 O G \\ \therefore & O A+O B+O C+O G \\ \Rightarrow & 3 O G+O G=4 O G \end{array}$$

content: If the volume of the parallelopiped whose conterminus edges are along the vectors $$\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}$$ is 12, then the volume of the tetrahedron whose conterminus edges are $$\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}$$ and $$c+a$$ is
explanation:
We have,

$$a, b, c$$ are coterminus edges of parallelopiped

$$\therefore$$ volume of parallelopiped $$=[\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{c}]$$

$$\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}$$ and $$\mathbf{c}+\mathbf{a}$$ are coterminus edges of tetrahedron

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \text { Volume of tetrahedron }=\frac{1}{6}[\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b} \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c} \mathbf{c}+\mathrm{a}] \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{6} \times 2[\mathbf{a ~ b ~ c}] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{r} {[\because[a+b b+c c+a]=2[a b c]} \\ =\frac{1}{3}[a b c]=\frac{12}{3}=4 \quad[\because[a b c]=12] \end{array}$$

$$\therefore$$ Volume of tetrahedron $$=4$$ (unit)$$^3$$

content: For any non-zero vectors $$\mathbf{a}$$ and $$\mathbf{b}$$,
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} {[b \mathbf{a}} & \times \mathbf{b} \mathbf{a}] \\ & =[\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} \mathbf{a b}] \quad[\because[a b c]=[b \mathbf{c} a]=[c \mathbf{a} b] \\ & =(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}) \cdot(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}) \\ & =(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b})^2=|\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}|^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the vectors $$\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+m \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ are coplanar, then $$m=$$
explanation:
Given vector $$\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \quad \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+m \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ are coplanar

$$\therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 & m \end{array}\right|=0$$

$$\begin{array}{rr} \Rightarrow & 1(-m-3)-1(m-2)+1(3+2)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -m-3-m+2+5=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 m=4 \Rightarrow m=2 \end{array}$$

content: The angles between the lines $$\mathbf{r}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \text { and } \mathbf{r}=(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda^{\prime}(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}), \lambda, \lambda^{\prime} \in \mathbf{R}$$ is
explanation:
Given line are

$$\mathbf{r}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$

and $$\mathbf{r}=(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\lambda^{\prime}(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})$$

Here, $$\mathbf{b}_1=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\mathbf{b}_2=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Angle between lines is

$$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta & =\left|\frac{\mathbf{b}_1 \cdot \mathbf{b}_2}{\left|\mathbf{b}_1\right|\left|\mathbf{b}_2\right|}\right| \\ \therefore \quad \cos \theta & =\left|\frac{(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{1+1+4} \sqrt{4+1+1}}\right| \\ \cos \theta & =\frac{2+1-2}{6}=\frac{1}{6} \\ \therefore \quad & \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: In a quadrilateral $$ABCD, M$$ and $$N$$ are the mid-points of the sides $$A B$$ and $$C D$$ respectively. If $$\mathbf{A D}+\mathbf{B C}=t \mathbf{M N}$$, then $$t=$$
explanation:
Given,

In quadrilateral $$A B C D$$

$$M$$ and $$N$$ are the mid-point of side $$A B$$ and $$C D$$.

Let $$\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}$$ and $$\mathbf{d}$$ are position vector $$A, B, C$$ and $$D$$ respectively.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad M & =\frac{a+b}{2}, N=\frac{c+d}{2} \\ \therefore \quad A D & =d-a, B C=c-b \\ M N & =\frac{c+d}{2}-\frac{a+b}{2} \\ 2 M N & =(d-a)+(c-b) \\ 2 M N & =A D+B C \\ \therefore \quad t & =2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ ] \neq 0$$, then $$\frac{[\vec{a}\ +\vec{b}\ \vec{b}\ +\vec{c}\ \vec{c}\ +\vec{a}\ ]}{[\vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{a}\ ]}=$$
explanation:
To solve this problem, let's use the properties of scalar triple product and understand the vectors involved. The scalar triple product of three vectors, say $$\vec{X}, \vec{Y}, \vec{Z}$$, is denoted and defined as $$[\vec{X} \ \vec{Y} \ \vec{Z}] = \vec{X} \cdot (\vec{Y} \times \vec{Z})$$, and it represents the volume of the parallelepiped spanned by the vectors. This product is invariant under a cyclic permutation of the vectors but changes sign on swapping any two vectors.

Given that $$[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}] \neq 0$$, we need to find the value of $$\frac{[\vec{a}+\vec{b}\ \vec{b}+\vec{c}\ \vec{c}+\vec{a}]}{[\vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{a}]}$$. To simplify the numerator of this expression, we expand the scalar triple product as follows:

$$[\vec{a}+\vec{b}\ \vec{b}+\vec{c}\ \vec{c}+\vec{a}]=[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}]+[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{a}]+[\vec{a}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{c}]+[\vec{a}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{a}]+[\vec{b}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}]+[\vec{b}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{a}]+[\vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{c}]+[\vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{a}]$$

Notice that the scalar triple product involving two identical vectors ($$[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{a}], [\vec{a}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{c}], [\vec{a}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{a}], [\vec{b}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}], [\vec{b}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{a}], [\vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{c}]$$) equals zero because the volume of the parallelepiped collapses in these cases. Therefore, our expression simplifies to:

$$[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}]+[\vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{a}]$$

As mentioned, the scalar triple product is invariant under cyclic permutations, which means $$[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}] = [\vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{a}]$$. So, the expression further simplifies to:

$$2[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}]$$

Therefore, dividing this by $$[\vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{a}]$$ (which is equal to $$[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}]$$ because of cyclicity), we get:

$$\frac{2[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}]}{[\vec{b}\ \vec{c}\ \vec{a}]} = \frac{2[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}]}{[\vec{a}\ \vec{b}\ \vec{c}]} = 2$$

Thus, the correct answer is Option D: 2.

content: If the scalar triple product of the vectors $-3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}, 3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-7 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\lambda \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $7 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ is 272 then $\lambda=\ldots \ldots$
explanation:
Scalar triple product of the given vectors is 272.

$\therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-3 & 7 & -3 \\ 3 & -7 & \lambda \\ 7 & -5 & -3\end{array}\right|=272(\because$ scalar triple product of the vectors $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{c i s}[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}]$)

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-3(21+5 \lambda)-7(-9-7 \lambda)-3(-15+49)=272 \\ & \Rightarrow-63-15 \lambda+63+49 \lambda-102=272 \\ & \Rightarrow 34 \lambda-102=272 \\ & \Rightarrow 34 \lambda=374 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=11 \end{aligned}$$

content: $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are non-collinear vectors. If $\mathbf{c}=(x-2) \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{d}=(2 x+1) \mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}$ are collinear vectors, then the value of $x=\ldots \ldots$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given, } c=(x-2) a+b \text { and }\\ &\begin{array}{l} d=(2 x+1) a-b \text { are collinear } \\ \therefore \quad c=\lambda d \\ \Rightarrow \quad (x-2) \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}=\lambda[(2 x+1) a-b] \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-2}{2 x+1}=\frac{1}{-1}=\lambda \\ \Rightarrow \quad 2 x+1=-x+2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 3 x=1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{1}{3} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: For any non zero vector, a, b, c $\mathbf{a} \cdot[(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}) \times(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c})]=$ ..........
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text {We have, } a \cdot[(b+c) \times(a+b+c)]\\ &\begin{aligned} & =\mathbf{a} \cdot[(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}+\mathbf{c} \times \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{c} \times \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c} \times \mathbf{c})] \\ & =\mathbf{a} \cdot[(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{a})+(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})+(\mathbf{c} \times \mathbf{a})+(c \times b)] \\ & =\mathbf{a}[(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{a})+(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})+(\mathbf{c} \times \mathbf{a})-(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})] \\ & =a[(b \times a)+(c \times a)] \\ & =[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{ba}]+[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{c} a]=0+0=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $A, B, C$ and $D$ are $(3,7,4),(5,-2,-3),(-4,5,6)$ and $(1,2,3)$ respectively, then the volume of the parallelopiped with $A B, A C$ and $A D$ as the co-terminus edges, is .......... cubic units.
explanation:
We are given points

$$ A(3,7,4),\quad B(5,-2,-3),\quad C(-4,5,6),\quad \text{and} \quad D(1,2,3). $$

The volume of the parallelepiped formed by the edges at point A, namely $$\vec{AB},\ \vec{AC},\ \text{and}\ \vec{AD},$$ is given by the absolute value of the scalar triple product:

$$ \text{Volume} = \left| \vec{AB} \cdot (\vec{AC} \times \vec{AD}) \right|. $$

Below are the steps to compute the volume:

Compute the vectors:

$$ \vec{AB} = B - A = (5-3,\ -2-7,\ -3-4) = (2,\ -9,\ -7). $$

$$ \vec{AC} = C - A = (-4-3,\ 5-7,\ 6-4) = (-7,\ -2,\ 2). $$

$$ \vec{AD} = D - A = (1-3,\ 2-7,\ 3-4) = (-2,\ -5,\ -1). $$

Find the cross product $$\vec{AC} \times \vec{AD}:$$

Using the determinant formula:

$$ \vec{AC} \times \vec{AD} = \begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ -7 & -2 & 2 \\ -2 & -5 & -1 \end{vmatrix}, $$

we compute each component:

$$ \text{i-component}: \quad (-2)(-1) - (2)(-5) = 2 + 10 = 12. $$

$$ \text{j-component}: \quad -\Big[ (-7)(-1) - (2)(-2) \Big] = -\Big[ 7 + 4 \Big] = -11. $$

$$ \text{k-component}: \quad (-7)(-5) - (-2)(-2) = 35 - 4 = 31. $$

So,

$$ \vec{AC} \times \vec{AD} = (12,\ -11,\ 31). $$

Calculate the scalar triple product:

Take the dot product of $$\vec{AB}$$ and $$\vec{AC} \times \vec{AD}$$:

$$ \vec{AB} \cdot (\vec{AC} \times \vec{AD}) = (2)(12) + (-9)(-11) + (-7)(31). $$

Compute each term:

$$2 \times 12 = 24,$$

$$-9 \times -11 = 99,$$

$$-7 \times 31 = -217.$$

Adding these:

$$ 24 + 99 - 217 = -94. $$

Take the absolute value to get the volume:

$$ \text{Volume} = | -94 | = 94 \quad \text{cubic units}. $$

Thus, the volume of the parallelepiped is $$\boxed{94}$$ cubic units, which corresponds to Option B.

content: If the vectors $x \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{i}}+y \hat{\mathbf{j}}-z \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ are collinear then the value of $\frac{x y^2}{z}$ is equal
explanation:
Given, vectors $x \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{i}}+y \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ are collinear

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{x}{1}=\frac{-3}{y}=\frac{7}{-z}=k \\ & \Rightarrow x=k,-3=k y \text { and } 7=-k z \end{aligned}$$

Now, $\frac{x y^2}{z}=\frac{k\left(-\frac{3}{k}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{-7}{k}\right)}=\frac{\frac{9}{k}}{\frac{-7}{k}}=\frac{-9}{7}$

content: Which of the following is not equal to $\mathbf{w} \cdot(\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v})$ ?
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Key Idea Use }\\ &\begin{aligned} & (a \times b) \cdot c=(b \times c) \cdot a=(c \times a) \cdot b \\ & \therefore \quad \mathbf{w} \cdot(\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v})=-\mathbf{v} \cdot(\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{w}) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}$ anc $\mathbf{c}+\mathbf{a}$ are coterminous edges of a parallel opiped then its volume is ..........
explanation:
We have $\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}$ and $\mathbf{c}+\mathbf{a}$ are coterminous edge of a parallelopiped then its volume is $[\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b} \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c} \mathbf{c}+\mathbf{a}]$

$$=2[\mathbf{a} b \mathbf{c}]$$

content: If $\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q}$ and $\mathbf{r}$ are non-zero, non-coplanar vectors
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given, p, } \mathbf{q} \text { and } \mathbf{r} \text { are non-zero coplanar vectors. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \therefore[\mathbf{p}+\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{r} \mathbf{p}-\mathbf{q} \mathbf{q}-\mathbf{r}] \\ & =(\mathbf{p}+\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{r}) \cdot[(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{q}) \times(\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{r})] \\ & =(\mathbf{p}+\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{r}) .[\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{q}-\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{q} \times \mathbf{q}+\mathbf{q} \times \mathbf{r}] \\ & =(\mathbf{p}+\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{r}) \cdot[\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{q}-\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{r}+\mathbf{q} \times \mathbf{r}](\because \mathbf{q} \times \mathbf{q}=0) \\ & =\mathbf{p} \cdot(\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{q})-\mathbf{p} \cdot(\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{r})+\mathbf{p} \cdot(\mathbf{q} \times \mathbf{r}) \\ & +\mathbf{q} \cdot(\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{q})-\mathbf{q} \cdot(\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{r})+\mathbf{q} \cdot(\mathbf{q} \times \mathbf{r}) \\ & -\mathbf{r} \cdot(\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{q})-\mathbf{r} \cdot(\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{r})+\mathbf{r} \cdot(\mathbf{q} \times \mathbf{r}) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$=2[\mathbf {p} \mathbf {q} \mathbf {r}]-[\mathbf {q}\mathbf {p}\mathbf {r}]-[\mathbf {r}\mathbf {p}\mathbf {q}]$

$=[\mathbf {p} \mathbf {q} \mathbf {r}]+[\mathbf {p}\mathbf {q}\mathbf {r}]-[\mathbf {p}\mathbf {q}\mathbf {r}]$

$=2[\mathbf {p} \mathbf {q} \mathbf {r}]-[\mathbf {p}\mathbf {q}\mathbf {r}]=[\mathbf {p}\mathbf {q}\mathbf {r}]$
